bq.q: A colleague says their canvas app logic 'defines each step to reach the goal, trading simplicity for fine-grained control.' Which development style is that?
bq.why: Imperative development focuses on HOW: you define each step and gain control at the cost of complexity. Declarative focuses on WHAT result you want.

bq.q: You must store the signed-in user's DisplayName once at launch and read it from any screen. Which function creates that value?
bq.why: Set creates a GLOBAL variable readable anywhere in the app. UpdateContext is screen-scoped; Collect/Patch write to data sources/collections.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about Power Apps variables is true.

rows.s: A context variable is available only on the screen where UpdateContext created it.
UpdateContext can declare several context variables in a single call.
The Set function can declare multiple global variables in one call, separated by commas.
Global variables are generally the recommended default when in doubt.

bq.why: UpdateContext can set many variables at once and is screen-scoped; Set defines one global variable per call, and globals are the flexible default.

bq.q: Which of the following are genuine Power Apps variable mechanisms? (choose 2)
bq.why: Power Apps uses global variables (Set), context variables (UpdateContext) and collections. Environment variables belong to solutions/Dataverse, and there are no typed module constants in Power Fx.

bq.q: The same expensive CountRows(Filter(...)) drives the Visible of both an icon and a button. What is the more performant, maintainable fix?
o: Run it once in the screen OnVisible, store the boolean in a variable, and bind both controls to that variable
Duplicate the formula on each control's Visible property
Move the formula into the app OnStart only
Wrap each formula in a separate ForAll
bq.why: Computing once into a variable avoids duplicate network/app/data-source work and keeps the logic in one place.

bq.q: You copy controls that use UpdateContext({varShowPopUp:true}) from Screen1 to Screen2. What happens to varShowPopUp?
o: Each screen keeps its own independent value under the same name
Both screens now share one synchronized value
The paste fails because context names must be unique
The variable is promoted to a global automatically
bq.why: Context variables are scoped per screen, so identically named ones on different screens hold independent values.

bq.q: For reading the user's name repeatedly, which option is the MOST performant?
o: A global variable set once in OnStart
A context variable set on each screen's OnVisible
Calling User() inline wherever it's needed
Recomputing it in a collection per screen
bq.why: A global set once in OnStart avoids re-hitting the data source; a context variable still queries each time the page opens, and inline User() is the slowest.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement is true when a variable stores a boolean like varOutstandingExceeded.

rows.s: A control's Visible property accepts the boolean variable directly, needing no extra formula.
Power Apps infers the variable's type (Boolean) from the value you assign.
You must explicitly declare the variable's data type before using it.

bq.why: Power Apps types variables implicitly from the assigned value; a boolean variable binds straight to Visible.

bq.q: Set(varCounter, varCounter + 1) sits on a button's OnSelect. Before it is ever set, what is varCounter's starting value?
bq.why: An uninitialized number variable defaults to 0, so the first click makes it 1.

bq.q: After Set(varUser, User()), how do you show the user's email in a label?
bq.why: A variable can hold a whole record; you read its columns with dot notation, e.g. varUser.Email.

bq.q: A variable counts invoices via OnStart. A user adds an invoice but the count doesn't change. Why?
o: Variables don't auto-update; the value only changes if you recompute it or the app restarts
OnStart runs continuously and should refresh it
bq.why: Variables are not reactive to data-source changes; you must update them explicitly or on the next OnStart.

bq.q: Decide the default value of each uninitialized variable type.

rows.s: A text variable defaults to an empty string "".
A number variable defaults to 0.
A boolean variable defaults to true.

bq.why: Defaults are "" for text, 0 for number and false for boolean.

bq.q: When is the Patch function the RIGHT choice over an edit form in a canvas app?
o: When you must change data programmatically without user interaction, or the design has no form
Whenever you display records in a gallery
Only for reading a single record's columns
Any time you navigate between screens
bq.why: Patch updates data sources directly, ideal for repetitive/programmatic writes or designs without forms.

bq.q: Which function removes ALL records from a collection while keeping its columns defined?
bq.why: Clear empties a collection but preserves its column structure; Remove/RemoveIf target specific rows.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about Patch, Remove and collections is true.

rows.s: Remove deletes a record without asking the user to confirm.
Patch, Remove and RemoveIf can target a collection as well as a tabular data source.
Patch and Remove can each modify many records at once on their own.

bq.why: Patch/Remove act on one record; for many you loop with ForAll. Both work on collections and tables, and Remove gives no confirmation.

bq.q: You need to write each row of one table into another, one record per source row. Which approach fits?
o: ForAll looping a Patch/Remove, or Collect to copy rows between tables
bq.why: Bulk changes use ForAll (per-record Patch/Remove) or Collect to append each source row to a target table.

bq.q: In Patch(CustomerOrders, Defaults(CustomerOrders), {Status:"Pending"}), what does Defaults() do?
o: Signals Patch to CREATE a new record
bq.why: Passing Defaults(DataSource) as the base record tells Patch to create a new row; passing an existing record edits it.

bq.q: Decide whether each Patch column rule is true.

rows.s: Column names in Patch are case-sensitive.
You must supply all REQUIRED columns of the data source.
You can write a string value into a number column as long as it looks numeric.
You may set as many or as few OPTIONAL columns as you want.

bq.why: Patch needs correct casing, all required columns, and matching data types; optional columns are up to you.

bq.q: Which are valid sources for the values you pass into a Patch change record? (choose 2)
o: A control property such as Dropdown1.Selected.Value
The output of a formula like User().FullName
The name of the screen you are on
bq.why: Patch values can come from hardcoded literals, variables, control properties or formula outputs.

bq.q: Patch(CustomerOrders, ThisItem, {Region:"Asia", Country:"China"}) is run on an existing row. What happens to prior Region/Country values?
o: They are overwritten with the new values
They are appended to a history log
The row is duplicated with the new values
Patch is rejected because the row exists
bq.why: Passing an existing record edits it in place, overwriting the specified columns.

bq.q: You must empty a data source's records AND immediately reload a fresh set into a collection in one step. Which function is designed for that?
o: Clear then a separate Collect on a timer
bq.why: ClearCollect combines Clear and Collect in a single call, ideal for reloading a collection all at once.

bq.q: For a shopping-cart collection where the user may empty the cart WITHOUT immediately adding new items, which pattern fits best?
o: Clear now and Collect later as separate steps
bq.why: Multi-step scenarios (clear now, add later) use separate Clear and Collect; all-at-once reloads use ClearCollect.

bq.q: RemoveIf(CustomerOrders, true) is executed. What is the result?
o: Every record is deleted because the condition is always true
Nothing, because true is not a valid filter
Only the first record is deleted
The columns are removed but rows remain
bq.why: RemoveIf tests each row against the condition; a condition of true matches all rows, deleting them (no undo).

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about deleting data is true.

rows.s: Power Apps provides a built-in undo / recycle bin for records deleted via RemoveIf.
Setting the condition to true also works with the Filter function for troubleshooting.

bq.why: There is no undo in Power Apps for deletes; recovery depends on the data source. A true condition is a handy Filter debug trick.

bq.q: Where are the values of a Dataverse choice column stored, and who can change them?
o: As table column metadata, editable only by an app maker
As ordinary rows any app user can edit
In a local collection on each screen
bq.why: Choice values live in column metadata and can only be modified by a maker, not end users.

bq.q: You create a Choice column and leave 'Sync with global choice?' at its default. What is that default and its effect?
o: Yes, so the same choices can be reused in other columns and tables
No, so the choices stay local to this one column
Yes, but the list becomes read-only forever
No, and multiple selection is disabled
bq.why: The default is Yes (global), letting the choice set be reused elsewhere; local (No) restricts it to that column/table.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about choice columns is true.

rows.s: A choice column can be configured to allow multiple selected values.
You should use global choices unless the values apply to a single column in a single table.
For a local choice, you must define each label and Power Apps assigns it an integer value.
End users can add new options to a choice list at run time.

bq.why: Choices support single/multiple selection, integer-backed labels, and global reuse; only makers edit the list.

bq.q: Before creating a choice column, what should most influence your decision to use a choice vs a lookup?
o: How the apps, automation and reporting will consume the data
The color you want each option to display
The number of screens in the app
Whether the app is tablet or phone layout
bq.why: Choose choice vs lookup based on how the data will be used downstream by apps, flows and reports.

bq.q: Why must you write Category = 'Category (Accounts)'.'Preferred Customer' rather than Category = 'Preferred Customer'?
o: The choice is an option-set/table, so you need the fully qualified table.column reference
Short names only work in galleries
Power Fx forbids spaces in values
bq.why: A choice is itself an option set; comparing to a bare text gives an 'Incompatible types' error, so use the full reference.

bq.q: A combo box lets users pick several categories. Which formula shows gallery rows matching ANY selected category?
bq.why: The in operator filters on membership across the multiple selected items.

bq.q: To also show ALL rows when no category is selected, what do you add to Filter(Accounts, Category in ComboBoxCategory.SelectedItems ...)?
bq.why: Adding || IsEmpty(...SelectedItems) returns all rows when the user has selected nothing.

bq.q: Filtering on a multi-select 'choices' column raises a delegation warning. What is a recommended workaround?
o: Create a Dataverse view that filters the choices, then filter against that view
Increase the row limit to 2000 and ignore it
Store the choices in a global variable first
Convert the column to a text field
bq.why: Because 'choices' is stored as a comma-separated list (non-delegable), a Dataverse view that pre-filters avoids the warning.

bq.q: What does the Choices() function return when set as a combo box's Items for a lookup/choice column?
o: A table of the possible values built from the column's metadata
A single text string of the default value
bq.why: Choices() prepares the list of possible values (a table) from the column metadata for selection.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about filtering choice columns is true.

rows.s: Forgetting the table prefix in a choice comparison yields an 'Incompatible types for comparison' error.
A Dataverse view workaround still lets the app user type the filter value at run time.
Choice and choices columns give consistent, standardized ways to filter rows.

bq.why: The view approach avoids delegation but fixes the criteria (no runtime user input); missing the prefix errors out.

bq.q: In a Dataverse one-to-many (1:N) relationship, where does the lookup column live and how many parents can a child have?
o: On the child table, and a child associates with exactly one parent
On the parent table, and a child can have many parents
On both tables, with unlimited parents
On neither table; it is inferred at run time
bq.why: 1:N places the lookup on the child (related) table; each child row points to a single parent row.

bq.q: A developer says the relationship is 'N:1'. How does that relate to a 1:N relationship?
o: It is the same physical relationship viewed from the child pointing to the parent
It is a completely separate many-to-many relationship
It requires an intersect table to exist
It only works in model-driven apps
bq.why: N:1 (many-to-one) is the same 1:N relationship seen from the child's angle.

bq.q: You need a lookup that can point to EITHER an account or a contact row. Which Dataverse feature enables this?
o: A multi-table (polymorphic) lookup, e.g. a Customer data type
bq.why: Multi-table (polymorphic) lookups, like the Customer type, let one column reference rows from several tables.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about Dataverse relationships is true.

rows.s: Dataverse supports one-to-many and many-to-many relationships.
A one-to-many relationship's child can be associated with only one parent.
Activity tables' regarding column can point to any activity-enabled table.

bq.why: Dataverse offers 1:N and N:N; a 1:N child has one parent; activity regarding lookups are polymorphic.

bq.q: Given a 1:N relationship, which is the SIMPLER way to list a selected location's desks instead of Filter()?
o: A nested ForAll over both tables
Adding the desk table as a second data source
bq.why: Dot notation across a 1:N relationship (Selected.Desks) returns related rows concisely; you can still add Filter for extra criteria.

bq.q: From a desk (child) row, how do you show its location's address using relationship navigation?
bq.why: You can navigate the many-to-one from child to parent with dot notation: ThisItem.Location.Address.

bq.q: Using Patch to set a lookup column on a desk, what value must you assign to the Location column?
o: A record from the primary (Location) table
The integer ID as a string
bq.why: Patch sets a lookup by assigning a whole record from the primary table, e.g. {Location: FilterLocation_1.Selected}.

bq.q: Which pair of functions associates and disassociates related rows without Patch?
bq.why: Relate() links rows across a relationship; Unrelate() removes the association.

bq.q: When establishing a MANY-TO-MANY relationship with Relate(), why doesn't the parameter order matter?
o: An N:N relationship has no primary table, so neither side is the parent
Relate always sorts its parameters alphabetically
N:N requires exactly one record on each side
bq.why: N:N is symmetric with no primary table, so which record is first/second is irrelevant.

bq.q: Adding an N:N field to a form throws errors on submit. Which workaround is described?
o: Clear the Update property and manually create the associations after submit
Convert the relationship to a choices column
bq.why: For N:N on a form you clear Update and use Relate() logic (e.g. on the check icon) to associate the saved selections after submitting.

bq.q: You must record HOW LONG a contact has spoken each language, not just that they speak it. What design handles this?
o: A custom intersect table with N:1 relationships to Contact and Language plus its own columns
A single choices column on Contact
bq.why: A pure N:N can't store attributes about the association; a custom intersect table (with N:1 links) can hold extra data like duration/proficiency.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement comparing choices columns and N:N relationships is true.

rows.s: Choices values are predetermined by the maker and can't be secured or disabled per row.
Rows in an N:N related table can be secured, deactivated and added at run time.
An N:N relationship by itself can store extra attributes about the association.

bq.why: Choices are static/maker-defined; N:N related rows are runtime-flexible but the relationship alone stores no extra data.

bq.q: Gallery1 shows customers; Gallery2 should show only the selected customer's invoices. What Items formula on Gallery2 works?
bq.why: Filtering the child table by the selected parent's ID shows just that customer's invoices.

bq.q: From an invoice (child) you know only CustomerID and want the customer's NAME. Which function retrieves that single parent record?
bq.why: LookUp returns the single matching record from a data source, letting you read the parent's columns.

bq.q: Showing a raw CustomerID to users adds little value. What is the better display approach for the gallery?
o: Use LookUp on the parent table to display the customer name instead of the ID
Convert the ID column to a choice
Store the ID in a global variable
bq.why: Replace the ID with a friendly value by looking up the related parent record's name.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about parent/child navigation in Power Apps is true.

rows.s: The parent Customer table needs no direct reference to the child Invoice table for the filter to work.
Text(ThisItem.InvoiceDate, DateTimeFormat.ShortDate) formats an invoice date for display.

bq.why: The child holds the foreign key, so the parent needs no reference; Text() with a DateTimeFormat renders dates.

bq.q: In the exercise, which function adds the selected gallery item into the cart collection colCart?
bq.why: Collect appends a record (ThisItem) to the collection.

bq.q: At check-out the code loops every cart item to create a line item per product. Which function performs that loop?
bq.why: ForAll iterates the colCart table, running Collect for each record to build ReceiptLineItems.

bq.q: The pattern If(IsEmpty(ReceiptHeader), 1, Last(ReceiptHeader).ID + 1) is used to do what?
o: Generate an auto-incrementing ID for the new record
bq.why: It creates a simple autonumber: 1 when empty, otherwise the last ID plus one.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about the relational cart exercise is true.

rows.s: LookUp(Products, ID=ThisItem.ProductID).Name resolves a line item's product name.
Filter(ReceiptLineItems, ReceiptHeaderID=Gallery3.Selected.ID) links a receipt's lines to the selected header.
Clear(colCart) is unnecessary after submitting an order.

bq.why: LookUp resolves names, Filter ties line items to their header, and Clear(colCart) empties the cart after check-out.

bq.q: In Power Automate, what is the main purpose of an expression?
o: To run functions that return/transform data beyond simple dynamic content
To assign security roles to a flow
To store a flow's run history
bq.why: Expressions use functions to calculate, convert and compare values when dynamic content alone isn't enough.

bq.q: Why do Power Automate expression docs often send you to Azure Logic Apps documentation?
o: Flows run on top of Logic Apps and share the same function reference
Power Automate has no functions of its own kind
bq.why: Power Automate runs on Azure Logic Apps, so both use the same functions and the reference lives on the Logic Apps docs.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about writing expressions is true.

rows.s: Function names in expressions are NOT case-sensitive.
You can open the expression editor with the fx button or by typing '/' in an input.
A '?' after a parameter (e.g. format?) marks it as required.

bq.why: Function names are case-insensitive; fx or '/' opens the editor; a trailing '?' marks an OPTIONAL parameter.

bq.q: A simple setup to test expressions without other inputs typically uses which trigger/action pair?
o: A manual trigger with a Compose action
A Dataverse trigger with a Condition
bq.why: 'Manually trigger a flow' + Compose is the recommended minimal harness for trying expressions.

bq.q: formatNumber(12.5,'C') is used to display a value. What does the 'C' specify?
bq.why: 'C' is the standard Currency format string, turning 12.5 into e.g. $12.50; an optional locale can change the symbol.

bq.q: length('I love Power Automate.') returns 22. Why 22 and not fewer?
o: Spaces are counted as characters too
bq.why: length counts every character in the string, including spaces and punctuation.

bq.q: Evaluate if(greater(12,10),'Yes','No'). What is returned and why?
o: 'Yes', because greater(12,10) is true and if returns its first value
true, because greater returns a boolean
an error, because you can't nest functions
bq.why: Read inside-out: greater(12,10) is true, so if returns the first value 'Yes'.

bq.q: Besides an expression, what built-in flow action lets you write If logic without typing an expression?
bq.why: The Condition action provides If/branching visually, an alternative to an if() expression.

bq.q: When you drag dynamic content into a flow, which kind of function is Power Automate writing for you behind the scenes?
bq.why: Dynamic content is backed by referencing functions like triggerOutputs()?['headers']?[...], written automatically.

bq.q: coalesce(null, 'Power Automate', 'Power Apps') returns which value?
bq.why: coalesce returns the first non-null value from the list.

bq.q: Which function returns the ID of the current flow run, useful for logging?
bq.why: The workflow() function exposes run metadata; workflow().run.id is the current run's ID.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about expression categories is true.

rows.s: uriQuery('https://x/?Test=Yes') returns the query-string portion (?Test=Yes).
A complex expression nests functions, using one function's output as another's input.
add(add(12,13),15) is invalid because functions cannot be nested.

bq.why: URI parsing extracts URI parts; complex expressions nest functions (e.g. add(add(12,13),15) = 40).

bq.q: What does 'delegation' mean for a canvas app data query?
o: The processing (filter/sort) is delegated to the data source instead of the device
The app copies all rows locally first
The user delegates the query to an admin
Power Fx runs the query twice for accuracy
bq.why: Delegation pushes filtering, sorting and aggregation to the data source so only needed rows are returned.

bq.q: Why is delegation important for apps over large data sources?
o: It avoids the client-side record limit by letting the source process all rows
It encrypts the data in transit
It lets you skip writing formulas
It converts the source to a collection
bq.why: Without delegation, only the first N rows (the delegation limit) are processed locally, risking incomplete results.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about delegation is true.

rows.s: Whether a function is delegable depends on both the function and the data source.
The column type involved can also affect whether an operation is delegable.
Delegation support is identical across Dataverse, SharePoint and SQL.

bq.why: Delegation depends on the function, the data source and sometimes the column type; support varies by connector.

bq.q: You must choose a data source for an app that will grow to tens of thousands of rows. What should you weigh most?
o: Its delegation support for the functions you need
Whether it has an emoji picker
The number of screens in the app
bq.why: Choosing a source with strong delegation support for your functions keeps large-data apps correct and fast.

bq.q: A blue underline/warning appears under part of your Filter formula. What is Power Apps telling you?
o: That part isn't delegable and may not return all matching rows
The formula has a syntax error
The app exceeded its storage quota
bq.why: A delegation warning flags a non-delegable operation that only processes rows up to the delegation limit.

bq.q: When a query isn't delegable, how many records are processed locally by default, and what's the maximum you can set?
o: Default 500, up to a maximum of 2000
bq.why: The non-delegable record limit defaults to 500 and can be raised to a maximum of 2000 in app settings.

bq.q: Which is the SAFEST way to handle a genuinely non-delegable function you must use?
o: Restructure so delegable operations narrow the data first, or ensure results stay under the limit
Always raise the limit to 2000 and ignore the warning
bq.why: Reduce the working set with delegable operations first; simply raising the limit still risks missing rows beyond 2000.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about delegation limits is true.

rows.s: A non-delegable function silently returns only the rows within the record limit.
Some functions are partially delegable depending on how they're used.
Raising the record limit guarantees you always get every matching row.

bq.why: Non-delegable functions cap at the limit; some are partially supported; raising the cap to 2000 still isn't unlimited.

bq.q: According to the module, what is the MOST common performance bottleneck in canvas apps?
o: The data sources and how the app calls them
The number of labels on a screen
bq.why: Data-source access patterns (excessive/inefficient calls) are the most common bottleneck.

bq.q: Which are cited as data-source performance problems? (choose 2)
o: Too many refreshes of the data
Too many lookups against the source
bq.why: Excessive refreshes and repeated lookups are common performance drains; so is storing data in the wrong source.

bq.q: Storing data in the 'wrong' data source hurts performance mainly because...
o: The source may not delegate the operations your app needs
bq.why: A poorly matched source can force non-delegable, slow client-side processing.

bq.q: What is a recommended mindset for improving app performance?
o: Rewrite the whole app at once
bq.why: The module advises optimizing performance incrementally in small steps.

bq.q: How can a collection improve performance when the same data is read repeatedly?
o: Cache the data once in a collection and read from memory instead of re-querying
Convert it to a choice column
bq.why: Caching stable data in a collection avoids repeated network round-trips.

bq.q: When is caching data in a collection appropriate?
o: When the data is relatively stable during the session
When the data changes every second
bq.why: Cache data that won't change while in use; frequently changing data would go stale.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about collections and performance is true.

rows.s: Reading from an in-memory collection is generally faster than re-hitting the data source.
Cached collection data can become stale if the source changes.

bq.why: Collections are fast local caches but do not auto-refresh from the source.

bq.q: Which function loads a fresh set of records into a collection in a single call?
bq.why: ClearCollect empties and repopulates a collection in one step, ideal for (re)caching.

bq.q: Which tool lets you watch your app's live network calls to diagnose performance?
bq.why: Monitor traces events including network activity, revealing slow or excessive calls.

bq.q: In Monitor, seeing many repeated calls to the same data source usually indicates...
o: An opportunity to cache or reduce redundant queries
That the app is working optimally
bq.why: Repeated identical calls signal redundant work you can cache or consolidate.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about diagnosing performance is true.

rows.s: Monitor can be used while the app runs to inspect each operation.
Network activity in Monitor helps spot excessive data-source calls.
You must publish the app before Monitor can show anything.

bq.why: Monitor attaches to a running session (including in Studio) and surfaces network/events live.

bq.q: After identifying a slow screen, what is a sound first optimization?
o: Reduce and cache the data the screen loads
Add more controls to the screen
bq.why: Cutting and caching the data a screen requests attacks the most common bottleneck directly.

bq.q: Which combination BEST keeps a large-data app both correct and responsive?
o: Delegable queries plus caching stable reference data
Raising the limit and caching everything forever
Disabling delegation and looping with ForAll
bq.why: Delegation keeps results complete; caching stable data cuts redundant calls.

bq.q: A gallery only shows 500 of thousands of rows. What's the root cause to investigate first?
o: A non-delegable operation in the gallery's Items
bq.why: Hitting exactly the delegation limit points to a non-delegable Items formula.

bq.q: Decide whether each performance guideline is true.

rows.s: Fewer, delegable data calls beat many client-side operations.
Concurrent loading can reduce total startup wait.

bq.why: Delegable, consolidated and concurrent data access all improve responsiveness.

bq.q: Which statement about the delegation limit is accurate?
o: It bounds how many rows non-delegable operations process locally
It limits how many screens an app can have
It caps the number of collections
It sets the app's frame rate
bq.why: The limit (500 default, 2000 max) governs local processing of non-delegable queries only.

bq.q: Where should you begin when tackling a canvas app that is slow to start?
o: Measure the app's load time to find what to optimize
bq.why: You first evaluate/measure load time so optimization targets the real cost.

bq.q: What does showing a loading image at startup accomplish?
o: Improves perceived performance while data loads
bq.why: A loading image improves perceived responsiveness; it doesn't change real load time.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about startup performance is true.

rows.s: Connector limits and throttling can slow data calls at startup.
Reviewing app settings is part of tuning startup.
Measuring load time is unnecessary before optimizing.

bq.why: Throttling/limits affect startup; you review settings and measure before optimizing.

bq.q: Throttling by a connector means...
o: Requests beyond a rate are delayed or rejected
bq.why: Throttling limits how many calls succeed in a window, delaying/blocking excess requests.

bq.q: Which app setting review most directly affects data correctness on large tables?
bq.why: The data row limit governs how many rows non-delegable queries return.

bq.q: Which preview/experimental settings should you review for performance?
bq.why: Data-loading/delegation settings are the performance-relevant ones to review.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about app settings is true.

rows.s: The data row limit can be raised to a maximum of 2000.
Lowering the row limit can reduce memory use when appropriate.

bq.why: The limit tops out at 2000; a smaller limit can reduce work if you don't need many rows locally.

bq.q: A slow OnStart is often caused by...
o: Doing too much sequential data work before the first screen shows
bq.why: Heavy sequential OnStart work delays the first render.

bq.q: Which is preferred over setting the app's initial screen with Navigate in OnStart?
o: Set the app's StartScreen property instead of Navigate in OnStart
bq.why: Using StartScreen avoids the overhead/limitations of Navigate during startup.

bq.q: Why move per-screen data loads from App.OnStart into each screen's OnVisible?
o: So each screen only loads its own data when shown, spreading the cost
So the app never loads data
bq.why: OnVisible defers a screen's data work until that screen is actually displayed.

bq.q: Which function loads several independent data sets in parallel to cut startup time?
bq.why: Concurrent runs multiple formulas in parallel rather than one after another.

bq.q: Decide whether each startup-optimization statement is true.

rows.s: OnVisible is better than OnStart for data only one screen needs.
Loading data once and reusing it avoids repeated calls.
Concurrent forces data to load strictly one item at a time.

bq.why: Defer with OnVisible, load once, and use Concurrent to parallelize (not serialize).

bq.q: Which data-load strategy keeps working data in memory to avoid repeated source calls?
o: Direct data source binding on every control
Storing it in the app title
bq.why: Preloading into a collection caches data for fast reuse.

bq.q: 'Direct data source binding' means...
o: A control reads straight from the source each time it needs data
Data is cached in a variable
Data is loaded once at install
bq.why: Direct binding queries the source on demand; good for fresh data, costlier for repeated reads.

bq.q: Which strategy persists data on the device so it survives app restarts?
bq.why: SaveData/LoadData persist data locally across sessions (offline-friendly).

bq.q: Decide whether each data-loading statement is true.

rows.s: Preloading into a variable suits a single reused value.
Direct binding always gives the freshest data but may cost more calls.
Persisted storage is best when data must always be real-time.

bq.why: Variables cache single values; direct binding is fresh but chatty; persisted storage trades freshness for offline availability.

bq.q: How does Monitor help teams debug an app together?
o: You can share a Monitor session so others watch the same live trace
bq.why: Monitor sessions can be shared so collaborators inspect the same live events.

bq.q: A Monitor trace shows an operation taking a long duration. What have you found?
o: A concrete performance hotspot to optimize
bq.why: Duration per event pinpoints the slow operation to fix.

bq.q: Decide whether each Monitor statement is true.

rows.s: Monitor records the sequence and timing of app operations.
Monitor can be used during Studio play, not only in a published app.

bq.why: Monitor captures event order/timing and works in Studio and published apps.

bq.q: Which is a common problem Monitor helps you identify and resolve?
bq.why: Monitor surfaces redundant/slow calls so you can consolidate or cache them.

bq.q: What is Application Insights used for with Power Apps?
o: Collecting telemetry (usage, performance, errors) over time
bq.why: Application Insights (Azure Monitor) captures app telemetry for ongoing diagnostics.

bq.q: How do Monitor and Application Insights differ in scope?
o: Monitor is a live debugging trace; Application Insights logs telemetry over time
bq.why: Monitor is real-time/interactive; Application Insights is longitudinal telemetry.

bq.q: Decide whether each telemetry statement is true.

rows.s: Application Insights can capture unhandled errors from the app.
You connect an app to Application Insights via an instrumentation key/connection string.
Application Insights replaces the need to ever measure load time.

bq.why: App Insights captures errors/telemetry via an instrumentation key but complements, not replaces, load-time analysis.

bq.q: You need historical trends of how many users hit a slow screen last week. Which tool fits?
bq.why: Historical trends are Application Insights' domain; Monitor is for the current session.

bq.q: How can Power BI complement app telemetry?
o: By visualizing Application Insights/usage data in reports
bq.why: Power BI can report on telemetry/usage data to surface trends visually.

bq.q: Which pairing makes the most sense for ongoing app health monitoring?
o: Application Insights feeding Power BI dashboards
bq.why: Telemetry in Application Insights visualized in Power BI gives ongoing health insight.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement is true.

rows.s: Power BI can build dashboards over telemetry exported from Application Insights.
Power BI is where you write the app's Power Fx.

bq.why: Power BI visualizes data (including telemetry); Power Fx is authored in Power Apps.

bq.q: Which end-to-end approach best sustains performance for a growing app?
o: Measure with Monitor, log with Application Insights, and act on the findings
bq.why: Measure (Monitor), log (App Insights) and iterate is the sustainable loop.

bq.q: OnStart sets several global variables the whole app uses. Is that appropriate?
o: Yes for truly app-wide values, but keep OnStart lean
bq.why: App-wide values belong in OnStart, but keep it minimal to protect startup time.

bq.q: A single screen's gallery data is loaded in App.OnStart. Better practice?
o: Move it to that screen's OnVisible
Put it in the app title
Load it in every screen's OnVisible
bq.why: Screen-specific data should load in that screen's OnVisible, not globally at startup.

bq.q: Decide whether each best practice is true.

rows.s: Keeping OnStart minimal improves time-to-first-screen.
Concurrent can parallelize independent OnStart loads.
Setting StartScreen is worse than Navigate in OnStart.

bq.why: Lean OnStart, Concurrent parallelization and StartScreen all help startup.

bq.q: Which report tool is best suited to visualize app usage/telemetry trends for stakeholders?
bq.why: Power BI builds shareable dashboards over telemetry/usage data; Monitor is only a live per-session trace.

bq.q: Which set correctly lists core Microsoft Power Platform products?
o: Power Apps, Power Automate, Power BI, Power Pages, Copilot Studio
Azure Functions, Logic Apps, Service Bus
bq.why: Power Platform comprises Power Apps, Power Automate, Power BI, Power Pages and Copilot Studio over Dataverse.

bq.q: Which Power Platform product is designed to build EXTERNAL-facing websites over Dataverse data?
bq.why: Power Pages builds secure external websites on Dataverse; the others target apps, automation, analytics and copilots.

bq.q: As a developer, when should you write CODE rather than use configuration?
o: Only when configuration can't meet the requirement
Always, code is faster to maintain
bq.why: The guidance is to prefer configuration and reserve code for what configuration can't achieve.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement about Power Platform for developers is true.

rows.s: Power Automate automates processes with cloud flows.
Power BI is primarily for building data visualizations and reports.
Copilot Studio is used to package managed solutions.

bq.why: Automate = flows, Power BI = analytics, Copilot Studio = conversational copilots (not solution packaging).

bq.q: What is the role of a connector in Power Platform?
o: A proxy that lets apps/flows talk to an external service or API
bq.why: Connectors expose external services/APIs to Power Platform through their operations.

bq.q: Which describes the difference between standard and premium connectors?
o: Premium connectors require a premium license/plan; standard ones are broadly included
Standard connectors need code; premium don't
bq.why: Premium connectors (e.g., many enterprise/SQL/custom) require premium licensing; standard connectors are more broadly available.

bq.q: When no built-in connector exists for your API, what can a developer build?
bq.why: Custom connectors wrap arbitrary REST APIs when no prebuilt connector exists.

bq.q: Decide whether each connector statement is true.

rows.s: Connectors have actions and (optionally) triggers.
A single connector definition can back many connections.

bq.why: Connectors expose actions/triggers; each authenticated connection instantiates the connector.

bq.q: Dataverse provides a security model built on which primary construct?
bq.why: Dataverse security uses roles granting privileges at table and record levels, with teams and business units.

bq.q: Which principle should guide privilege assignment in Dataverse?
o: Least privilege — grant only what's needed
bq.why: Apply least privilege: give the minimum access required for the task.

bq.q: Decide whether each security statement is true.

rows.s: Teams can be used to share access among multiple users.
Business units help organize security boundaries.
Security is enforced only in the UI, not the API.

bq.why: Teams/business units structure access; Dataverse enforces security at the platform (API) level, not just UI.

bq.q: The Common Data Model (CDM) primarily provides...
o: Standardized table and column definitions with shared semantics
bq.why: CDM standardizes entities/attributes so data is consistent and interoperable.

bq.q: Why would a developer 'consume' the Common Data Model?
o: To reuse standard schemas instead of reinventing table structures
bq.why: Consuming CDM reuses well-defined standard schemas, saving design effort and aiding interoperability.

bq.q: Which is a benefit of storing business data in Dataverse?
o: Built-in security, relationships, and rich metadata across apps
It removes the need for any UI
bq.why: Dataverse offers security, relationships, business logic and metadata usable across the platform.

bq.q: Decide whether each CDM/Dataverse statement is true.

rows.s: Dataverse can work with many data types and app types.
CDM promotes consistent semantics across solutions.

bq.why: Dataverse is broad and metadata-driven; CDM standardizes meaning across solutions.

bq.q: Which extensibility layer changes the USER EXPERIENCE of a model-driven app?
bq.why: UX extensibility uses client script and PCF; plug-ins/Service Bus are server/integration-side.

bq.q: Which Azure services support large-scale analytics over Dataverse data?
o: Azure Data Lake and Azure Synapse Analytics (via Synapse Link)
bq.why: Azure Synapse Link exports Dataverse data to a Data Lake for analytics in Synapse.

bq.q: Synapse Link for Dataverse is mainly used to...
o: Continuously export Dataverse data for analytics without impacting the transactional store
bq.why: Synapse Link replicates Dataverse data to a lake so analytics run separately from the operational store.

bq.q: Decide whether each analytics statement is true.

rows.s: Azure Synapse Analytics can query data exported from Dataverse.
A Data Lake is a suitable target for large analytical datasets.
You must copy data manually every day for Synapse Link to work.

bq.why: Synapse Link automates continuous export to a Data Lake queryable by Synapse.

bq.q: A developer must choose where to add complex server-side business logic. Which is the platform's data+logic core?
bq.why: Dataverse is the data and server-logic core (business rules, plug-ins, security) of Power Platform.

bq.q: What is the Microsoft Power Platform CLI (pac) primarily used for?
o: Command-line scaffolding and management of PCF, solutions and auth
Editing canvas app screens by drag-and-drop
bq.why: pac CLI scaffolds PCF/plug-in projects and manages solutions, environments and authentication.

bq.q: Which tooling integrates Power Platform development into Visual Studio / VS Code?
bq.why: Power Platform Tools for Visual Studio/VS Code bring CLI and project support into the IDE.

bq.q: Decide whether each developer-tooling statement is true.

rows.s: NuGet packages exist for building Dataverse plug-ins/assemblies.
There is a dedicated Connector CLI for custom connectors.
The CLI cannot authenticate to an environment.

bq.why: NuGet supplies Dataverse SDK packages; a Connector CLI exists; pac auth manages environment sign-in.

bq.q: Which command initializes and helps build a PCF component from the CLI?
bq.why: pac pcf init scaffolds a code-component project; npm build then compiles it.

bq.q: In the CLI exercise, what is the FIRST task before creating a component?
bq.why: You install the Power Platform CLI before scaffolding or using components.

bq.q: After building a PCF component, how do you get it into an environment during development?
o: Push it with the CLI (pac pcf push) into the Dataverse environment
Add it to the app title
bq.why: pac pcf push deploys the component to the environment for testing during development.

bq.q: Decide whether each CLI statement is true.

rows.s: npm build compiles the component's TypeScript/resources.
The CLI can create a solution project for packaging.

bq.why: npm build compiles the component; pac solution init creates a solution project.

bq.q: Which command family manages solutions from the CLI?
bq.why: pac solution handles init, packing, exporting and importing solutions.

bq.q: How do managed and unmanaged solutions differ in intent?
o: Unmanaged for development; managed for downstream (test/prod) deployment
Managed for development; unmanaged for production
bq.why: You develop in unmanaged and deploy managed to target environments.

bq.q: 'Solutions can be layered' means...
o: Multiple solutions stack, and the top layer determines the effective behavior
Each solution deletes the previous one
Only one solution can ever exist
Layers apply only to canvas apps
bq.why: Solution layering stacks changes; the topmost active layer wins for a component.

bq.q: Decide whether each solution statement is true.

rows.s: Solution dependencies must be present in the target to import successfully.
Managed solutions are typically used to deploy to production.
Unmanaged solutions are the recommended production artifact.

bq.why: Dependencies must exist in the target; managed is the production artifact, unmanaged is for dev.

bq.q: A component won't import because a required table it uses is missing in the target. What is the cause?
bq.why: Missing prerequisites are unmet solution dependencies that block import.

bq.q: Which tool converts a solution .zip into source-control-friendly files (and back)?
bq.why: SolutionPackager / pac solution unpack decomposes a solution for source control; pack rebuilds it.

bq.q: Why unpack a solution into individual files for source control?
o: To track and merge component changes and enable proper ALM
bq.why: Unpacked files enable diffing, merging and versioning for healthy ALM.

bq.q: Decide whether each packaging statement is true.

rows.s: A solution can be packaged as managed or unmanaged.
Packaging tools support automation in build pipelines.

bq.why: You can pack managed/unmanaged and automate packaging in CI/CD (Build Tools).

bq.q: Which combination best supports a professional developer workflow?
o: CLI + source control + SolutionPackager for repeatable ALM
bq.why: CLI, source control and SolutionPackager together give repeatable, automatable ALM.

bq.q: Which two mechanisms extend the USER EXPERIENCE of Power Apps?
o: Power Apps component framework (PCF) and client scripting
bq.why: PCF and client scripting extend the UX; plug-ins/Azure services extend server/integration.

bq.q: Client scripting extends which app type's forms with event-driven logic?
bq.why: Client scripts (JavaScript) run on model-driven form events via the Client API.

bq.q: Decide whether each extensibility statement is true.

rows.s: PCF components can be reused across multiple apps.
Client script reacts to form and field events.
UX extensibility is the right place for heavy server transactions.

bq.why: PCF is reusable UI; client script handles form events; heavy server logic belongs in plug-ins.

bq.q: Which describes when UX extensibility is the wrong tool?
o: When logic must run reliably server-side regardless of the client
When you need a custom control
When you react to a field change
When you validate on the form
bq.why: Client-side logic can be bypassed; server-side rules (plug-ins) enforce reliably.

bq.q: Which two APIs let external/server code work with Dataverse?
o: The Web API and the Organization Service
The Graph API and OneDrive API
The Bing API and Maps API
The Excel API and Word API
bq.why: Dataverse exposes the OData Web API and the .NET Organization Service.

bq.q: Where do plug-ins execute within Dataverse?
o: On the event (execution) pipeline stages for a message
bq.why: Plug-ins register on pipeline stages (pre-validation, pre/post-operation) for messages like Create/Update.

bq.q: Decide whether each Dataverse extensibility statement is true.

rows.s: The Organization Service is a .NET SDK for Dataverse operations.
The Web API is a RESTful OData v4 endpoint.
Plug-ins can only run asynchronously.

bq.why: Org Service (.NET) and Web API (OData) both access Dataverse; plug-ins run sync or async.

bq.q: The event pipeline stage that runs BEFORE the main operation and can validate/cancel is...
bq.why: Pre-validation and pre-operation run before the database operation; post-operation runs after.

bq.q: What is a Custom API in Dataverse?
o: A reusable, definable operation (message) callable by clients and code
bq.why: A Custom API defines a new bound/unbound Dataverse message with typed input/output parameters.

bq.q: A Custom API can be invoked from...
o: Power Automate, the Web API, and plug-in/code callers
bq.why: Once defined, a Custom API is a first-class message callable from flows, Web API and code.

bq.q: Decide whether each Custom API statement is true.

rows.s: A Custom API can be bound to a table or unbound (global).
A Custom API can define input and output parameters.

bq.why: Custom APIs are bound or unbound and declare typed request/response parameters.

bq.q: Which pairing correctly matches a UX vs a server extension?
o: Custom API = UX; client script = server
Power BI = UX; Web API = client
bq.why: Client script/PCF extend UX; plug-ins/Custom APIs extend server logic.

bq.q: For simple declarative field logic (e.g., set/lock a field), which should you try FIRST?
bq.why: Prefer business rules for simple declarative logic before writing client script or plug-ins.

bq.q: Logic must run reliably on the server whenever a record is created, even via the API. Choose:
bq.why: Only server-side code (a plug-in) runs regardless of the client, including API writes.

bq.q: Decide whether each configure-vs-code guideline is true.

rows.s: Prefer flows/business rules over plug-ins when they meet the need.
Client script can be bypassed by non-form data entry paths.
You should always write a plug-in even for trivial UI logic.

bq.why: Favor low-code where sufficient; remember client script isn't enforced server-side; don't over-code trivial UI logic.

bq.q: A calculation must combine data and run for both UI and background updates. Best fit?
o: A plug-in (server-side) so it applies to every write path
bq.why: Server-side plug-in logic applies uniformly across UI and background/API operations.

bq.q: How are client script files stored and deployed in a model-driven app?
bq.why: Client scripts are uploaded as JavaScript web resources referenced by forms.

bq.q: Your script calls functions from a shared helper library. What must you configure so it loads?
o: A web resource dependency on the library
bq.why: Declaring web resource dependencies ensures dependent libraries load before your script runs.

bq.q: Decide whether each client-scripting statement is true.

rows.s: Event handlers can be registered from a form's properties.
Event handlers can also be registered in code (addOnChange, etc.).
Client script is authored in C#.

bq.why: Handlers register via form properties or code (addOnChange/addOnSave); client script is JavaScript.

bq.q: Which form events commonly trigger client scripts?
bq.why: OnLoad, OnSave and OnChange are the core form/field events for client scripts.

bq.q: What does the execution context passed to a handler primarily give you?
o: Access to the form context (data and UI) for the current record
bq.why: The execution context exposes getFormContext() to reach the form's data and UI.

bq.q: Which object do you use to read/write a column's value on the form?
bq.why: getAttribute returns the attribute whose getValue/setValue read and write the column.

bq.q: Which retrieves a control (not the data) to change UI, e.g. hide it?
bq.why: getControl returns the control for UI operations like setVisible/setDisabled.

bq.q: Decide whether each context statement is true.

rows.s: Passing the execution context to functions is the recommended pattern.
You can navigate tabs and sections through the form context.
getAttribute changes a control's visibility.

bq.why: Pass the execution context; use getControl (not getAttribute) for visibility; tabs/sections are reachable via the form context.

bq.q: To hide a form SECTION when a field is empty, which approach is correct?
o: getControl on the section (via tab.sections) and call setVisible(false)
Delete the section from the table
bq.why: Client script toggles section visibility with setVisible via the tab/section objects.

bq.q: Which is the recommended way to make a field required from client script?
bq.why: setRequiredLevel controls requirement level at runtime from client script.

bq.q: Decide whether each common-task statement is true.

rows.s: You can show/hide tabs as well as sections.
addOnChange registers a handler for a field's change event.

bq.why: Tabs and sections both support visibility; addOnChange wires a field's OnChange.

bq.q: The exercise prepares which artifact first so the script can be added to a form?
bq.why: You prepare a solution with the form so the web resource and handlers can be added.

bq.q: Why pass the execution context as the first parameter to your handler function?
o: So the function can call getFormContext() without relying on globals
bq.why: Passing the context lets the function obtain the form context in a supported, reliable way.

bq.q: A script runs on OnLoad but should also react when a user edits a field. What do you add?
o: An OnChange handler for that field
bq.why: Field edits fire OnChange; register a handler there in addition to OnLoad.

bq.q: Decide whether each statement is true.

rows.s: Web resources can include libraries your main script depends on.
Handlers may be registered both declaratively and in code.
Client script always runs on the server.

bq.why: Web resources host libraries; handlers register declaratively or in code; client script runs on the client.

bq.q: A field's value must drive whether another control is enabled. Which pair do you use?
o: getAttribute to read the value, getControl to setDisabled
getControl to read the value, getAttribute to setVisible
bq.why: Read data with getAttribute; change UI (enable/disable) with getControl.setDisabled.

bq.q: Which Xrm object performs CRUD against Dataverse from client script?
bq.why: Xrm.WebApi provides createRecord/retrieveRecord/updateRecord/etc. from client script.

bq.q: Which Xrm object opens forms, dialogs and navigates the app?
bq.why: Xrm.Navigation handles openForm, openAlertDialog, navigateTo and similar UX navigation.

bq.q: Decide whether each global Xrm statement is true.

rows.s: Xrm.Device can access device capabilities like location or camera.
Xrm.Encoding provides string/HTML encoding helpers.
Xrm.WebApi is only for opening dialogs.

bq.why: Device/Encoding provide device and encoding helpers; WebApi is for data operations, not dialogs.

bq.q: Which Xrm namespace offers utility helpers such as getGlobalContext and lookups?
bq.why: Xrm.Utility exposes helpers like getGlobalContext and lookup dialogs.

bq.q: For simple declarative field logic, which is preferred over client script?
bq.why: Business rules handle simple declarative logic without code; prefer them when sufficient.

bq.q: Logic that must run reliably even when data is created outside the form should be...
o: A business rule on the form only
bq.why: Client script (and form business rules) don't run for non-form data paths; use a plug-in.

bq.q: Decide whether each best-practice statement is true.

rows.s: You should run the Solution Checker to catch issues in customizations.
Define unique function names to avoid collisions across libraries.
Using deprecated/unsupported methods is recommended for brevity.

bq.why: Use Solution Checker, unique names, and avoid deprecated/unsupported methods.

bq.q: Two libraries both define a function named 'onLoad'. What problem can occur?
o: A naming collision where one overwrites the other
bq.why: Global name collisions cause one function to overwrite the other; use unique/namespaced names.

bq.q: Which is a supported way to debug client script?
o: Browser developer tools with breakpoints on the web resource
bq.why: Use the browser dev tools (sources/breakpoints, console) to debug the loaded web resource.

bq.q: Before shipping customizations, running the Solution Checker helps you...
o: Detect performance, reliability and deprecated-API issues
bq.why: Solution Checker statically analyzes for problems including deprecated APIs and best-practice violations.

bq.q: Decide whether each debugging statement is true.

rows.s: console.log output can be inspected in the browser console.
You can set breakpoints in the loaded JavaScript web resource.

bq.why: Browser dev tools show console output and support breakpoints in the web resource.

bq.q: A calculated column already gives the value you need on the form. Should you also script it?
o: No — avoid duplicating logic; use the calculated column
Yes — always add client script too
Only if you write a plug-in as well
bq.why: Don't duplicate logic; a calculated column (or business rule) is simpler than redundant script.

bq.q: You must run extra validation specifically on save. Which event do you handle?
bq.why: Save-time validation belongs in the OnSave handler (which can also prevent the save).

bq.q: In OnSave, how can client script prevent an invalid save?
bq.why: preventDefault() on the save event args cancels the save from client script.

bq.q: Decide whether each advanced-scripting statement is true.

rows.s: Xrm.WebApi calls are asynchronous and return promises.
Coding standards recommend unique, namespaced function names.
OnSave cannot cancel a save.

bq.why: WebApi is async/promise-based; use namespacing; OnSave can cancel via preventDefault.

bq.q: The exercise's client script is added to which artifact so it runs on the form?
o: A form event handler referencing the JavaScript web resource
bq.why: You register the web resource's function as a form/field event handler.

bq.q: What is the Power Apps component framework (PCF) used to build?
o: Reusable code components that extend app UI beyond standard controls
bq.why: PCF builds reusable code components (custom controls) for model-driven and canvas apps.

bq.q: A key ADVANTAGE of PCF over HTML web resources is that components...
o: Bind to data/properties and are reusable and configurable across apps
bq.why: PCF components are data-bound, configurable and reusable, unlike ad-hoc web resources.

bq.q: Decide whether each PCF statement is true.

rows.s: PCF components can be used in both model-driven and canvas apps.
Creator Kit and community components can accelerate building UIs.
PCF is intended for server-side transactions.

bq.why: PCF is client-side UI; usable in both app types; Creator Kit/community components help.

bq.q: Which types of components can you create with PCF?
bq.why: PCF supports field (single value) and dataset (grid/collection) components.

bq.q: Which CLI tool scaffolds and builds PCF projects?
bq.why: The Power Platform CLI (pac pcf init, then build) scaffolds and builds components.

bq.q: Which IDE is commonly used to develop PCF components with the CLI?
bq.why: VS Code (with the CLI/Node tooling) is the typical PCF development environment.

bq.q: Decide whether each tooling statement is true.

rows.s: Node.js/npm are used to build PCF components.
The CLI can create a solution to package the component.

bq.why: PCF builds rely on Node/npm; pac solution packages the component for deployment.

bq.q: The PCF component lifecycle methods include which sequence?
bq.why: PCF implements init (setup), updateView (render on change), getOutputs (return values) and destroy (cleanup).

bq.q: Where are a component's inputs, outputs and resources declared?
bq.why: The manifest declares properties (inputs/outputs), types and resource files.

bq.q: Which manifest concept defines the data a field component is bound to?
o: A property (of a specific data type)
bq.why: Manifest properties define bound values and their types for the component.

bq.q: Decide whether each manifest/property statement is true.

rows.s: Properties can be input, output or bound.
The manifest specifies each property's data type.

bq.why: Manifest properties declare direction/binding and data types for the component.

bq.q: When a bound value changes, which lifecycle method is called to re-render?
bq.why: updateView runs whenever data/context changes so the component re-renders.

bq.q: Which statement best captures why teams choose PCF over ad-hoc scripting?
o: Reusability, typed properties and a defined lifecycle make components maintainable
bq.why: PCF's typed properties, lifecycle and reusability yield maintainable, shareable UI.

bq.q: What is the FIRST step to create a code component from the CLI?
o: Install the Power Platform CLI (and Node.js)
bq.why: You install the CLI (and Node.js) before scaffolding a PCF project.

bq.q: Which command scaffolds a new PCF component project?
bq.why: pac pcf init creates the component project structure.

bq.q: Decide whether each project-setup statement is true.

rows.s: npm install restores the project's dependencies.
The manifest is edited to declare properties before coding logic.
PCF projects require no package manager.

bq.why: npm restores dependencies; you edit the manifest; PCF relies on npm.

bq.q: After adding a new property to the component, what must you do so the typings update?
o: Rebuild (npm build) to regenerate the manifest typings
bq.why: Rebuilding regenerates the strongly typed ManifestTypes used in code.

bq.q: Where do you write the component's rendering/behavior logic?
bq.why: index.ts implements the lifecycle methods (init/updateView/getOutputs/destroy).

bq.q: How do you add CSS styling to a code component?
o: Add a CSS resource and reference it in the manifest
bq.why: Styles are added as CSS resources referenced from the manifest.

bq.q: Decide whether each logic/styling statement is true.

rows.s: updateView is where you read context and render output.
getOutputs returns values back to the app/framework.
destroy is called when the component initializes.

bq.why: updateView renders, getOutputs returns values, destroy runs on teardown (not init).

bq.q: Which command compiles the component during development?
bq.why: npm run build (or npm start for the harness) compiles the TypeScript/resources.

bq.q: Which tool lets you run and debug the component locally without deploying?
o: The Power Apps component test harness (npm start)
bq.why: npm start launches the local test harness to run/debug the component.

bq.q: To deploy the component to a Dataverse environment during development, you...
bq.why: pac pcf push deploys the built component to the environment for testing.

bq.q: Decide whether each build/test statement is true.

rows.s: The test harness simulates context inputs and data inputs.
You can iterate: change code, rebuild, and re-run the harness.

bq.why: The harness feeds context/data inputs and supports rapid rebuild-run cycles.

bq.q: For proper ALM, how should a finished component be delivered?
bq.why: Components ship inside solutions for versioning and deployment (ALM).

bq.q: Which command creates a solution project to package the component?
bq.why: pac solution init creates a solution project; you then add the component reference.

bq.q: After pac solution init, how do you include the component in the solution?
o: pac solution add-reference to the component project
Add it to the app title
bq.why: pac solution add-reference links the component project into the solution.

bq.q: Decide whether each packaging statement is true.

rows.s: Building the solution project produces an importable solution.
The solution can be built managed for deployment.
A component cannot be part of a solution.

bq.why: Building the solution project yields a solution (managed/unmanaged) containing the component.

bq.q: Context inputs vs data inputs in the harness differ how?
o: Context inputs simulate the runtime context; data inputs supply the bound data values
bq.why: The harness separates simulated context from the actual bound data values you test with.

bq.q: A component renders once but never updates when the field changes. Which method likely isn't handling changes?
bq.why: updateView must react to context/data changes to re-render on updates.

bq.q: Decide whether each end-to-end statement is true.

rows.s: The typical flow is init -> build -> test harness -> push -> package.
npm build regenerates manifest typings after property changes.

bq.why: Scaffold, build, test, push and package is the standard PCF flow; rebuild refreshes typings.

bq.q: Which command builds the solution project into a deployable solution file?
o: dotnet build (or msbuild) on the solution project
bq.why: Building the .cdsproj solution project (dotnet build/msbuild) produces the importable solution .zip.

bq.q: Decide whether each solution-packaging statement is true for a code component.

rows.s: The built solution can be imported into another environment.
You can produce a managed build for deployment.
A code component cannot be shipped in a solution.

bq.why: The solution build yields an importable artifact (managed or unmanaged) that carries the component.

bq.q: In Dataverse, what are plug-ins implemented as?
bq.why: Plug-ins are .NET assemblies (classes implementing IPlugin) registered on Dataverse events.

bq.q: Dataverse is described as 'metadata-driven and solution-aware'. What does that enable?
o: Customizations are tracked as components that move via solutions
Plug-ins run only in canvas apps
bq.why: Being metadata-driven/solution-aware lets customizations be packaged and transported via solutions.

bq.q: Decide whether each Dataverse development statement is true.

rows.s: Plug-ins can extend both model-driven apps and other clients that write to Dataverse.
The extensibility model exposes APIs for code to interact with Dataverse.
Dataverse cannot be extended with .NET code.

bq.why: Dataverse extensibility supports .NET plug-ins/APIs affecting any write path to the platform.

bq.q: Which is a benefit of Dataverse's extensibility model for developers?
o: Server-side logic that applies regardless of the client
It removes the need for security
bq.why: Server-side extensions run for every client, enforcing logic consistently.

bq.q: In the event framework, what flows through the pipeline for an operation like Create?
o: An event message with stages you can register steps on
bq.why: Messages (Create/Update/Delete...) traverse pipeline stages where plug-in steps register.

bq.q: Which pipeline stage runs AFTER the main database operation?
o: None; there is no post stage
bq.why: Post-operation runs after the core operation; pre-validation/pre-operation run before it.

bq.q: Decide whether each event-framework statement is true.

rows.s: Synchronous steps run immediately and can affect the operation's outcome.
Asynchronous steps run in the background via the system job queue.
Pre-validation runs after post-operation.

bq.why: Sync runs inline (can block/alter), async runs in background; pre-validation is earliest, not last.

bq.q: You need to STOP an invalid Create before it is committed. Which execution mode/stage fits?
o: A synchronous pre-operation (or pre-validation) plug-in that throws
bq.why: A synchronous pre-stage plug-in can throw to cancel the operation before commit.

bq.q: Before writing a plug-in, which alternative should you consider first for simple logic?
bq.why: Prefer low-code alternatives (business rules, flows) when they meet the requirement.

bq.q: A plug-in class must implement which interface?
bq.why: Plug-ins implement IPlugin.Execute(IServiceProvider).

bq.q: Decide whether each plug-in scenario statement is true.

rows.s: Plug-ins suit complex, reusable server-side logic tied to data events.
A workflow/flow may be a better choice than a plug-in for some automation.
Client script can enforce rules server-side.

bq.why: Plug-ins fit complex server logic; flows suit some automation; client script isn't server-enforced.

bq.q: Which reason justifies a plug-in over a business rule?
o: The logic is complex and must run on every write path
You want to change a field color
bq.why: Complex, always-on server logic is a plug-in's domain; business rules are for simple declarative cases.

bq.q: In the plug-in execution context, where is the incoming data (e.g. Target) found?
bq.why: InputParameters holds inputs like the Target entity for the message.

bq.q: What are PreEntityImage and PostEntityImage used for?
o: Capturing a row's values before and after the operation
bq.why: Entity images snapshot attribute values before/after the operation for comparison.

bq.q: Decide whether each execution-context statement is true.

rows.s: OutputParameters carries values returned by the operation (e.g. the new id).
SharedVariables pass data between plug-ins in the same pipeline execution.
A PreImage is available only in the post-operation with no configuration.

bq.why: OutputParameters returns results; SharedVariables share across the pipeline; images must be registered/configured.

bq.q: You need the OLD value of a field being updated to compare against the new one. What do you register?
bq.why: A PreImage captures pre-update values for comparison with the incoming Target.

bq.q: In the exercise, how do you verify the plug-in behaves correctly?
o: Trigger the registered message and test the resulting data/behavior
bq.why: You test by performing the operation the step is registered on and checking results.

bq.q: A plug-in formats a phone number on create. Which stage keeps the formatted value on the saved row most simply?
bq.why: Modifying the Target in pre-operation writes the formatted value as part of the same operation.

bq.q: Decide whether each plug-in registration statement is true.

rows.s: You register a step specifying the message, table and stage.
Images are configured with the specific attributes you need.

bq.why: Steps specify message/table/stage; images are configured with required attributes.

bq.q: Which tool is commonly used to register plug-in assemblies and steps?
bq.why: The Plug-in Registration Tool registers assemblies, steps and images.

bq.q: The Dataverse Web API is best described as...
o: An OData v4 RESTful endpoint over HTTP
bq.why: The Web API is an OData v4 REST endpoint accessible over HTTP from any platform.

bq.q: When would you choose the Organization Service over the Web API?
o: For .NET code that benefits from the strongly typed SDK
For a Python script with no SDK
bq.why: The Organization Service (.NET SDK) suits .NET apps; the Web API suits any HTTP/OData client.

bq.q: Decide whether each Web API vs Org Service statement is true.

rows.s: The Web API is platform-independent because it is HTTP/OData.
The Organization Service is a .NET SDK.
FetchXML cannot be used to express queries.

bq.why: Web API is cross-platform OData; Org Service is .NET; FetchXML expresses complex queries.

bq.q: Which query language expresses complex Dataverse queries (joins, aggregates) in XML?
bq.why: FetchXML is Dataverse's XML query language for complex queries.

bq.q: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
o: Authentication proves who you are; authorization determines what you may do
Authorization proves identity; authentication grants roles
Both only apply to the UI
bq.why: Authn verifies identity; authz decides permitted actions/resources.

bq.q: To call the Dataverse Web API from an app, where do you register that app?
bq.why: You register the client app in Microsoft Entra ID and grant Dataverse API permissions.

bq.q: Decide whether each auth statement is true.

rows.s: The Web API uses OAuth 2.0 with bearer access tokens.
You configure API permissions for the registered app.
Basic username/password headers are the recommended Web API auth.

bq.why: Dataverse Web API uses OAuth 2.0 bearer tokens via Entra ID app registration, not basic auth.

bq.q: Which library family helps acquire tokens for Dataverse from code?
bq.why: MSAL handles OAuth token acquisition for Microsoft identity platform APIs like Dataverse.

bq.q: Before code, which tool can you use to test Web API requests interactively?
bq.why: REST clients like Insomnia/Postman let you test Web API calls and tokens before coding.

bq.q: How do you find the current version of your environment's Web API endpoint?
bq.why: The Web API URL includes the version segment (e.g. /api/data/v9.2); the environment exposes it.

bq.q: Decide whether each testing statement is true.

rows.s: A generated access token is sent as a bearer token in the Authorization header.
You must test the connection before trusting query results.

bq.why: Requests carry the OAuth bearer token; verifying the connection first is good practice.

bq.q: An OData query returns 400 due to a malformed $filter. What have you likely done?
o: Used incorrect OData query option syntax
bq.why: Malformed OData options (e.g. $filter/$select) cause 400 Bad Request errors.

bq.q: How can you determine WHICH user performed an operation from server code?
o: Read the initiating/user id from the execution context or WhoAmI
bq.why: The execution context (UserId/InitiatingUserId) or WhoAmI reveals the acting user.

bq.q: The WhoAmI message/request returns...
o: The calling user's id (and business unit/org ids)
bq.why: WhoAmI returns the current user's UserId along with BusinessUnitId and OrganizationId.

bq.q: Decide whether each Web API usage statement is true.

rows.s: $select limits which columns are returned.
$filter restricts which rows are returned.
The Web API cannot create or update rows.

bq.why: OData $select/$filter shape results; the Web API supports full CRUD.

bq.q: Which combination correctly secures and calls the Dataverse Web API?
o: Entra ID app registration + OAuth token + OData request
bq.why: Register in Entra ID, obtain an OAuth token, then issue OData HTTP requests.

bq.q: You must integrate a non-.NET service with Dataverse over HTTP. Which access method fits best?
bq.why: Non-.NET/HTTP clients use the Web API; the Org Service is a .NET SDK.

bq.q: Decide whether each closing statement is true.

rows.s: Authorization can be scoped by the API permissions granted to the app.
OData and FetchXML are both ways to query Dataverse.

bq.why: App permissions scope authorization; OData and FetchXML are both query options.

bq.q: In an audited scenario you must record WHICH user changed a row from server code. Which value is authoritative?
o: The user id from the execution context / WhoAmI
bq.why: The execution context user id (or WhoAmI) authoritatively identifies the acting user.

bq.q: Decide whether each 'who did it' statement is true.

rows.s: InitiatingUserId can differ from UserId when code impersonates a user.
WhoAmI returns the current user's id.
There is no way to know which user performed an operation.

bq.why: Impersonation makes InitiatingUserId vs UserId differ; WhoAmI returns the caller's id; the acting user is always discoverable.

bq.q: Which Azure service reliably relays Dataverse messages to external systems via queues/topics?
bq.why: Azure Service Bus queues/topics decouple and reliably deliver Dataverse messages to listeners.

bq.q: Which Azure service is designed for high-throughput event streaming/telemetry ingestion?
bq.why: Event Hubs ingests large-scale event streams; Service Bus is messaging; Event Grid routes discrete events.

bq.q: Decide whether each Azure integration statement is true.

rows.s: Event Grid routes discrete events to handlers using a publish-subscribe model.
Logic Apps can orchestrate integrations similar to cloud flows.
API Management is used to store binary files.

bq.why: Event Grid = pub/sub eventing; Logic Apps = orchestration; API Management is a gateway, not file storage.

bq.q: When choosing an Azure integration option, what should drive the decision?
o: The messaging pattern and requirements (reliability, ordering, scale)
bq.why: Pick the service by the integration pattern/requirements (reliable messaging vs eventing vs orchestration).

bq.q: To publish Dataverse data to Service Bus, what must you register in Dataverse?
o: A Service Bus endpoint (service endpoint) and a step
bq.why: You register a service endpoint pointing at Service Bus and a step to post messages on events.

bq.q: What consumes the messages Dataverse posts to Service Bus?
o: A listener/consumer application reading the queue or subscription
bq.why: An external listener reads the queue/subscription to process Dataverse messages.

bq.q: Decide whether each Service Bus integration statement is true.

rows.s: Queues deliver each message to a single consumer.
Topics/subscriptions support multiple subscribers (pub/sub).

bq.why: Queues are point-to-point; topics with subscriptions enable pub/sub fan-out.

bq.q: The Dataverse-to-Azure integration step posts the message at which point?
o: When the registered event (e.g. create/update) occurs
bq.why: The step fires on the registered event, posting the message to the endpoint.

bq.q: When should you use an Azure Function INSTEAD of a Dataverse plug-in?
o: For long-running, resource-heavy, or externally integrated logic
For simple pre-validation that must block a save
Never; plug-ins can do everything equally well
bq.why: Functions suit long-running/heavy/external work; plug-ins have execution-time limits and run in-process.

bq.q: A plug-in has a strict execution time limit. What is a common workaround for heavy processing?
bq.why: Offloading to a Function (often via Service Bus) avoids the plug-in's time constraints.

bq.q: Decide whether each Functions-vs-plug-ins statement is true.

rows.s: Plug-ins run within the Dataverse sandbox with time limits.
Azure Functions scale independently of Dataverse.
Azure Functions run inside the Dataverse event pipeline.

bq.why: Plug-ins are sandboxed/time-limited; Functions scale separately and run in Azure, not the pipeline.

bq.q: Which describes a good use of Azure with Dataverse for enterprise integration?
o: Publish events to Service Bus, process them in a Function
Store all UI in Event Hubs
bq.why: A common pattern: Dataverse publishes to Service Bus and an Azure Function processes the messages.

bq.q: What are Azure Functions?
o: Event-driven serverless code that scales on demand
bq.why: Azure Functions run small pieces of event-driven code serverlessly, scaling automatically.

bq.q: How do Azure Functions compare to Azure Logic Apps?
o: Functions are code-first; Logic Apps are designer/connector-first orchestration
Functions cannot be triggered by events
bq.why: Functions are code-centric; Logic Apps are low-code orchestrations with connectors.

bq.q: Decide whether each Functions statement is true.

rows.s: Functions can be triggered by HTTP, timers, queues and more.
WebJobs are an older approach that Functions build upon.
Functions must always run on a dedicated VM you manage.

bq.why: Functions support many triggers, evolved from WebJobs, and offer serverless (no VM management) plans.

bq.q: On the Consumption plan, what happens when there is no traffic?
o: The app can scale to zero (cold starts possible)
bq.why: Consumption scales to zero when idle, which can introduce cold starts.

bq.q: Which plan is designed to reduce cold starts while keeping serverless scaling?
bq.why: The Premium plan keeps pre-warmed instances to avoid cold starts while scaling elastically.

bq.q: Which hosting option runs Functions inside a containerized environment?
bq.why: Functions can run on Azure Container Apps for containerized hosting.

bq.q: Decide whether each hosting/scaling statement is true.

rows.s: The Flex Consumption plan offers serverless scale with more configuration than classic Consumption.
Dedicated (App Service) plan runs on instances you provision.
Function apps have no time-out considerations at all.

bq.why: Flex Consumption adds configurability; Dedicated uses provisioned instances; time-out duration varies by plan.

bq.q: A long-running function keeps hitting a time-out. What likely determines that limit?
o: The hosting plan's function time-out duration
