bq.why: Function time-out duration depends on the hosting plan (e.g., stricter on Consumption).

bq.q: How can you develop and test Azure Functions before deploying to Azure?
o: Run and debug them locally with the Functions tools
Only via the Dataverse form editor
bq.why: The Azure Functions Core Tools/VS Code let you run and debug functions locally.

bq.q: Which local file holds a function app's local app settings during development?
bq.why: local.settings.json stores local settings/connection strings for local runs.

bq.q: Decide whether each local-development statement is true.

rows.s: Local settings can be synchronized to the Azure app's application settings.
You can debug a function locally with breakpoints.
Local development requires a paid production plan.

bq.why: Settings can be synced to Azure; local debugging is supported; no production plan needed to develop locally.

bq.q: What starts a function executing?
bq.why: Every function has exactly one trigger that causes it to run.

bq.q: What is the role of a binding (vs a trigger) in Azure Functions?
o: It declaratively connects input/output data to the function
bq.why: Bindings connect data sources/sinks declaratively; the trigger starts execution.

bq.q: Which are the possible binding directions?
bq.why: Bindings are input (in), output (out), or in some cases inout.

bq.q: Decide whether each trigger/binding statement is true.

rows.s: A function has exactly one trigger.
A function can have multiple input and output bindings.
A trigger and a binding are the same thing.

bq.why: One trigger per function; multiple bindings allowed; triggers and bindings are distinct concepts.

bq.q: Which programming models are shown as examples for writing functions?
o: C# isolated worker, Node.js v4, and Python v2
bq.why: The module shows C# (isolated worker), Node.js (v4) and Python (v2) examples.

bq.q: What is the recommended way for a function to connect to Azure services WITHOUT secrets?
o: Hardcoding a connection string in code
Storing the password in a label
bq.why: Identity-based connections use a managed identity, avoiding stored secrets.

bq.q: After enabling a managed identity, what else is required for it to access a resource?
o: Granting the identity the appropriate permission/role on the resource
bq.why: You must grant the identity a role/permission on the target resource.

bq.q: Decide whether each connection statement is true.

rows.s: Identity-based connections reduce secret management burden.
You configure the identity and then grant it permissions.

bq.why: Managed identities remove secrets; you enable the identity and assign it permissions.

bq.q: In the exercise, which tool is used to create the Azure Function?
bq.why: The exercise creates an Azure Function using Visual Studio Code.

bq.q: A custom connector lets Power Platform talk to what?
o: A REST API not covered by an existing connector
bq.why: Custom connectors wrap arbitrary REST APIs so apps/flows can call them.

bq.q: Where can you create a custom connector in a low-code way?
bq.why: The maker portal creates custom connectors, ideally within a solution for ALM.

bq.q: Decide whether each connector-authoring statement is true.

rows.s: A connector exposes actions and optionally triggers.
Creating the connector inside a solution supports ALM.
Connector general information includes host and base URL.

bq.why: Connectors define actions/triggers, general info (host/base URL), and belong in solutions for ALM.

bq.q: The connector's 'general information' typically includes...
o: Host, base URL, scheme and description
bq.why: General information sets the API host, base URL, scheme and description.

bq.q: How are an operation's inputs and outputs defined in a custom connector?
o: Through its request and response definitions
bq.why: Request/response definitions describe the payloads (parameters, schema) of each operation.

bq.q: What does an action's 'visibility' setting control?
bq.why: Visibility (none/advanced/internal/important) governs how/whether the operation surfaces to makers.

bq.q: Decide whether each definition statement is true.

rows.s: Action and trigger naming affects how operations appear to makers.
You can add validation to catch definition errors.
A response definition is never needed.

bq.why: Naming shapes discoverability; validation catches issues; responses define output schemas.

bq.q: Setting 'internal' visibility on an action means it...
o: Is hidden from typical makers but still callable
bq.why: Internal operations are hidden from the standard maker experience but remain usable.

bq.q: In the exercise, how is the operation set defined quickly from an existing API description?
bq.why: Importing an OpenAPI definition auto-generates the connector's actions/definitions.

bq.q: After creating the connector, how do you verify it works?
o: Create a connection and test its operations
bq.why: You create a connection (authenticate) and test the operations from the Test tab.

bq.q: Decide whether each testing statement is true.

rows.s: You must create a connection before testing operations.
Adjusting request/response definitions can fix test failures.

bq.why: A connection provides auth for testing; tweaking definitions resolves schema/parameter issues.

bq.q: Once tested, how do you make the custom connector usable across environments?
o: Keep it in a solution to export/import via ALM
bq.why: Solution-based connectors move between environments through standard ALM.

bq.q: What is the difference between a connector and a connection?
o: A connector is the definition; a connection is an authenticated instance of it
A connection is the definition; a connector is the instance
A connection is a Dataverse table
bq.why: The connector defines operations; a connection is a specific authenticated use of it.

bq.q: In Visual Studio, how can you expose your web API to Power Platform quickly?
o: Add Power Platform as a Connected Service
Store it in Dataverse as a row
bq.why: The Connected Services experience in VS scaffolds a custom connector for your API.

bq.q: Decide whether each authoring-path statement is true.

rows.s: A custom connector can be created from an OpenAPI document.
A custom connector can be created from Azure API Management.
Custom connectors can only be built by hand in the portal.

bq.why: You can author from OpenAPI, from APIM, from VS Connected Services, or by hand.

bq.q: Which document format is commonly imported to define a connector's operations?
bq.why: OpenAPI (Swagger) describes the API and auto-populates connector operations.

bq.q: After adding Power Platform as a connected service in VS, what can you generate to try it?
o: An app that consumes the API via the connector
bq.why: The tooling can create an app from the API to preview the connector working.

bq.q: Why run your API first before finalizing the connector from Visual Studio?
o: So the tooling can read its endpoints/definition to build the connector
bq.why: Running the API lets the tooling discover its endpoints/OpenAPI to scaffold the connector.

bq.q: Decide whether each VS/connected-service statement is true.

rows.s: You can preview the generated app to validate the connector.
The connected service maps your API into a custom connector.

bq.why: Connected Services maps the API to a connector and you can preview an app to validate it.

bq.q: Web APIs placed 'behind Azure API Management' benefit from...
o: A managed gateway (security, throttling, versioning) in front of the API
bq.why: APIM provides a gateway offering security, rate-limiting, and versioning for backend APIs.

bq.q: How do you turn an API managed by APIM into a Power Platform connector?
o: Export the API from API Management to a custom connector
bq.why: API Management can export an API definition directly as a custom connector.

bq.q: After exporting from APIM, what must a maker do to actually call the connector?
bq.why: A connection authenticates the connector so apps/flows can invoke its operations.

bq.q: Decide whether each APIM statement is true.

rows.s: APIM can front multiple backend APIs behind one gateway.
Exporting from APIM speeds up creating a custom connector.

bq.why: APIM fronts backends and its export accelerates connector creation.

bq.q: Which end-to-end path is valid for surfacing a custom REST API in a flow?
o: Define via OpenAPI/APIM/VS -> custom connector -> connection -> use in flow
Add it as a Dataverse column
Put the URL in the app title
bq.why: Author the connector (OpenAPI/APIM/VS), create a connection, then use it in apps/flows.

bq.q: Which authentication type provides delegated, token-based security via Microsoft Entra ID for a connector?
bq.why: OAuth 2.0 with Entra ID delegates secure, token-based access.

bq.q: Which auth option sends a single secret token, often in a header, with no user sign-in?
bq.why: API key auth passes a static key/secret; there is no interactive sign-in.

bq.q: Decide whether each authentication statement is true.

rows.s: Basic authentication sends a username and password.
No authentication exposes the API without credentials.
OAuth 2.0 stores no tokens and needs no identity provider.

bq.why: Basic = user/pass; No auth = open; OAuth 2.0 relies on an identity provider and tokens.

bq.q: An API Management gateway in front of a connector's backend helps by...
bq.why: APIM centralizes cross-cutting concerns like security, throttling and routing.

bq.q: To use OAuth 2.0 with Microsoft Entra ID, you must first...
o: Register an app in Entra ID and configure its permissions
bq.why: Entra ID OAuth requires an app registration with configured API permissions/scopes.

bq.q: When configuring the connector for Entra ID OAuth, which values are typically required?
o: Client id, client secret, and authorization/token URLs (scopes)
bq.why: OAuth configuration needs client id/secret, auth/token endpoints and scopes.

bq.q: Decide whether each Entra ID configuration statement is true.

rows.s: A redirect URL may be required in the app registration.
Scopes/permissions define what the token can access.
Client secrets should be committed to source control.

bq.why: Redirect URLs and scopes are configured; secrets must never be committed to source control.

bq.q: Sharing a connector vs sharing a connection differs how?
o: Sharing a connection also shares its credentials/access; sharing a connector shares the definition
Sharing a connector shares your password
bq.why: A shared connection grants use of its credentials; sharing the connector shares only the definition.

bq.q: Which auth type is appropriate for a legacy on-prem service expecting domain credentials?
bq.why: Windows authentication suits services expecting Windows/domain credentials.

bq.q: For a public, read-only API that needs no credentials, which setting is valid?
bq.why: A truly open API can use 'No authentication'.

bq.q: Decide whether each choose-auth statement is true.

rows.s: OAuth 2.0 is generally preferred for secure, user-delegated scenarios.
API keys are simpler but must be protected as secrets.

bq.why: OAuth 2.0 is the secure default; API keys are simple but are secrets to protect.

bq.q: In the exercise, calling Microsoft Graph from a custom connector requires which auth?
bq.why: Microsoft Graph requires Entra ID OAuth 2.0 with appropriate scopes.

bq.q: The exercise creates the connector inside a solution mainly to...
bq.why: Solution membership enables ALM and moving the connector between environments.

bq.q: After configuring Entra ID auth, the final validation step is to...
o: Create a connection and test an operation (e.g. a Graph call)
bq.why: You create a connection (sign in) and test an operation to confirm the auth works.

bq.q: Decide whether each exercise statement is true.

rows.s: A new solution is created to hold the connector.
Testing requires an authenticated connection.
Graph can be called anonymously.

bq.why: The connector goes in a solution, testing needs a connection, and Graph needs authentication.

bq.q: Which is the MOST secure choice for a connector to a business API handling user data?
o: A shared API key in plain text
bq.why: OAuth 2.0 via Entra ID (tokens, scopes, no stored user passwords) is the most secure option.

bq.q: What are connector policies (policy templates) used for?
o: To transform requests/responses at runtime without writing code
bq.why: Policy templates modify traffic (headers, values, routing) declaratively, code-free.

bq.q: How can you apply and manage connector policies besides the UI?
o: Using the CLI to view and modify them
bq.why: Policies can be viewed and modified via the CLI in addition to the maker UI.

bq.q: Decide whether each policy statement is true.

rows.s: Policies can read runtime values via policy expressions.
Multiple policies can be applied to a connector.
Policies require writing a plug-in.

bq.why: Policies use expressions on runtime values and can be layered, all without plug-ins.

bq.q: A policy that needs the value of an incoming header would use...
o: A policy expression referencing the header
bq.why: Policy expressions access runtime values such as headers, query and body.

bq.q: Policy expressions can access which runtime values?
o: Headers, query string, body and connection values
bq.why: Expressions can read headers, query parameters, request body and connection values.

bq.q: Which is a valid use of a data-conversion policy?
o: Reshaping or converting request/response data formats
bq.why: Data-conversion policies transform payload data formats between client and backend.

bq.q: Decide whether each expression statement is true.

rows.s: Policies can read a value from the connection (e.g. a stored setting).
Policies can work with query string values.

bq.why: Policy expressions can reference connection values and query string parameters.

bq.q: Working with the response body in a policy lets you...
o: Transform or extract data returned by the backend
bq.why: Response-body policies transform/extract data returned to the caller.

bq.q: Which policy changes where the request is actually sent?
bq.why: Host URL and route-request policies redirect the outgoing request.

bq.q: The 'Set property' policy template is used to...
o: Add or update a value in the request/response
bq.why: Set property adds or updates values within the message.

bq.q: Decide whether each routing/values statement is true.

rows.s: You can set or update a query string parameter with a policy template.
Routing policies can send the call to a different host.
Policies cannot add values to a request.

bq.why: Templates set query strings and values and can reroute to a different host.

bq.q: The 'Set query string parameter' policy template lets you...
o: Add or modify a query parameter on outgoing requests
bq.why: It injects/updates a query string parameter on the request.

bq.q: Which is the biggest advantage of using policies vs custom code for these transforms?
o: No code to maintain; configured declaratively on the connector
They remove the need for auth
bq.why: Policies are declarative/no-code, reducing maintenance versus custom transform code.

bq.q: A backend expects an extra header your callers don't send. Which approach fits best?
o: A policy that sets the header on the request
bq.why: A set-value policy can inject the required header automatically.

bq.q: Decide whether each closing policy statement is true.

rows.s: Policies can transform both requests and responses.
Policy expressions enable dynamic, runtime-driven behavior.

bq.why: Policies act on requests and responses, driven dynamically by expressions.

bq.q: To apply a policy you generally...
o: Add the policy template to the connector and configure its parameters
bq.why: You add a policy template to the connector and set its parameters (no backend changes).

bq.q: What do OpenAPI extensions add to a custom connector?
o: Advanced behaviors like dynamic values, dynamic schema, test connection and chunking
bq.why: OpenAPI extensions enable dynamic values/schema, test connection and chunk transfer.

bq.q: Which editor lets you edit the connector's underlying definition directly?
bq.why: The Swagger editor edits the connector's OpenAPI (Swagger) definition.

bq.q: Decide whether each OpenAPI-extension statement is true.

rows.s: Extensions are expressed within the OpenAPI definition.
The Swagger editor exposes the raw definition for editing.
Extensions require a plug-in to function.

bq.why: Extensions live in the OpenAPI definition (editable in the Swagger editor); no plug-in needed.

bq.q: Enabling chunk transfer on an operation helps with...
o: Handling large payloads by transferring them in chunks
bq.why: Chunk transfer lets large content be sent/received in chunks.

bq.q: What does the 'dynamic list of values' extension do?
o: Populate a parameter's selectable options at runtime from the API
bq.why: Dynamic values fetch selectable options at runtime instead of hardcoding them.

bq.q: Configuring dynamic values requires pointing to...
o: An operation that returns the list, and the value/display fields
bq.why: You reference an operation returning the list and map its value/display properties.

bq.q: Decide whether each dynamic-values statement is true.

rows.s: Dynamic values improve UX by offering real options instead of free text.
They are configured through OpenAPI extensions.

bq.why: Dynamic values give real, current options and are set via extensions.

bq.q: What is the purpose of the 'test connection' extension?
o: Let the platform validate a connection is working
bq.why: Test connection defines how the platform verifies a connection is functional.

bq.q: The 'dynamic schema' extension is useful when...
o: The response/request shape varies based on the operation or inputs
bq.why: Dynamic schema adapts the payload structure to the chosen operation/inputs.

bq.q: Dynamic schema differs from dynamic values in that it...
o: Adapts the data STRUCTURE, not just a list of selectable values
bq.why: Dynamic values populate options; dynamic schema shapes the payload structure itself.

bq.q: Decide whether each dynamic-schema statement is true.

rows.s: Dynamic schema can reflect an operation-specific response shape.
It is configured through an OpenAPI extension.
It is the same as chunk transfer.

bq.why: Dynamic schema (via an extension) tailors payload structure; chunking is unrelated (large transfers).

bq.q: Not all APIs support every extension. What should you verify first?
o: The API's support/behavior for the extension you plan to use
bq.why: Extension behavior depends on API support; confirm compatibility before relying on it.

bq.q: In the exercise, extensions are added after which step?
o: Importing the solution/connector to work with
bq.why: You import the solution/connector, then add and configure the extensions.

bq.q: Configuring dynamic values for an 'invoice type id' parameter means users will...
o: Choose from a live list of invoice types instead of typing an id
bq.why: Dynamic values present the real invoice types to pick, improving accuracy/UX.

bq.q: Decide whether each exercise statement is true.

rows.s: You can add a test connection extension to validate connectivity.
Extensions are configured per operation/parameter as needed.

bq.why: You add test-connection and configure dynamic values/schema per operation/parameter.

bq.q: Which combination of extensions most improves a data-entry action's usability?
o: Dynamic values for pick-lists plus dynamic schema for the payload
Chunk transfer plus a new theme
Test connection plus a Power BI visual
bq.why: Dynamic values (real options) and dynamic schema (right structure) together improve data entry.

bq.q: What problem do connection references solve in solutions?
o: They decouple a flow/app from a specific connection so it can be rebound per environment
They store user passwords in the app
bq.why: Connection references let components bind to environment-appropriate connections during deployment.

bq.q: What are environment variables used for in solutions?
o: Externalizing configuration values that differ per environment
bq.why: Environment variables hold config (URLs, keys, ids) set per environment without editing components.

bq.q: Decide whether each solution-config statement is true.

rows.s: Environment variables avoid hardcoding environment-specific values.
Connection references separate the flow from a fixed connection.
Secrets should be hardcoded into flows.

bq.why: Env variables and connection references externalize config; secrets should not be hardcoded.

bq.q: Editing a solution-aware app or flow means...
o: It lives in a solution and moves with it through ALM
bq.why: Solution-aware components are packaged in the solution and transported via ALM.

bq.q: For deploying to a downstream (production) environment, which solution type is recommended?
bq.why: Managed solutions are the recommended artifact for downstream/production environments.

bq.q: Where should makers do active customization work?
o: In an unmanaged solution in a development environment
In a managed solution in production
bq.why: Development happens in unmanaged solutions in a dev environment; managed is for deployment.

bq.q: Decide whether each managed/unmanaged statement is true.

rows.s: Managed solutions can be uninstalled to remove their components.
Unmanaged customizations layer on the environment's default solution.
You should develop directly in a managed production solution.

bq.why: Managed can be uninstalled cleanly; unmanaged edits the default layer; don't develop in managed prod.

bq.q: A complex solution includes flows, apps and tables. When importing to a new environment you must ensure...
o: Its dependencies (and connection references/env variables) are satisfied
bq.why: Imports require dependencies present and connection references/env variables configured.

bq.q: Which Microsoft tooling automates solution export/import in a DevOps pipeline?
bq.why: Power Platform Build Tools provide pipeline tasks to automate ALM operations.

bq.q: Why put solutions under source control?
o: To track changes, review, and enable automated builds/releases
bq.why: Source control enables history, review and automated CI/CD for solutions.

bq.q: Decide whether each automation statement is true.

rows.s: You can automate exporting an unmanaged solution and importing it as managed.
Build pipelines can pack/unpack solutions.

bq.why: Pipelines can export unmanaged, build managed, and pack/unpack via Build Tools.

bq.q: Which is the healthiest ALM flow across environments?
o: Develop unmanaged in dev, export, deploy managed to test/prod via pipelines
Edit prod, then copy back to dev by hand
bq.why: Dev (unmanaged) -> managed deploy to test/prod through pipelines is the recommended ALM flow.

bq.q: In solution layering, which layer determines the effective behavior of a component?
o: The unmanaged base always overrides managed
bq.why: Managed layers stack over the unmanaged base; the top active layer determines behavior.

bq.q: How does a managed UPGRADE differ from a managed UPDATE?
o: An upgrade replaces the solution and removes components deleted in the new version; an update patches in place
An upgrade only changes the theme
bq.why: Upgrades fully replace (removing deleted components); updates/patches apply changes without removing.

bq.q: Decide whether each layering/ALM statement is true.

rows.s: pac solution unpack turns a solution into source-control-friendly files.
Version control helps manage solution changes over time.
The unmanaged base sits above all managed layers.

bq.why: Unpack aids source control; version control tracks changes; the unmanaged base is at the bottom, not top.

bq.q: Which practice best supports healthy long-term solution architecture?
o: Version-controlled, layered managed solutions with automated ALM
One giant unmanaged solution edited in production
Screenshots as the source of truth
bq.why: Layered managed solutions under version control with automated ALM is the healthy architecture.

zones.name: Hall of Variables
zones.sub: Imperative vs declarative - global/context variables - collections

enemies.lore: Global variables (Set) hold a value app-wide for the session.
Context variables (UpdateContext) are scoped to a single screen.
Collections store tables of records in memory via Collect/ClearCollect.

boss.lore: Imperative logic defines HOW step by step (Set, Navigate); declarative defines WHAT.

zones.name: Forge of Patch
zones.sub: Patch to create/edit records - Remove/RemoveIf - editing beyond forms

enemies.lore: Patch creates or edits records without needing a form control.
Patch with Defaults() creates a new record; passing a record edits it.
Remove and RemoveIf delete records; Remove does not ask to confirm.

boss.lore: Patch(DataSource, BaseRecord, ChangeRecord) writes precise changes to data.

zones.name: Gallery of Choices
zones.sub: Local vs global choices - choice vs lookup - filtering choices with Power Fx

enemies.lore: Local choices belong to one column; global choices are reusable across tables.
A choice stores a fixed option set; a lookup points to a row in another table.
Filter Dataverse choice columns in Power Fx using the option value/record.

boss.lore: Use global choices for reuse and lookups for real relationships between tables.

zones.name: Keep of Relations
zones.sub: 1:N and N:N relationships - related rows - relational data in Power Apps

enemies.lore: A one-to-many (1:N) relationship links a parent row to many child rows.
Many-to-many (N:N) relationships connect rows on both sides freely.
A lookup column on the child materializes its relationship to the parent.

boss.lore: Dataverse relationships (1:N, N:N) model related data; Power Apps navigates them.

zones.name: Sanctum of Expressions
zones.sub: Power Fx functions: string, logical, math, date/time, collection

enemies.lore: The formula bar offers auto-suggest, hints and links as you type Power Fx.
Conversion functions (Value, Text, DateValue) change a value's type.
Combine functions (If, With, ForAll) to write complex expressions.

boss.lore: Power Fx is the declarative, Excel-like typed expression language of Power Apps.

zones.name: Bastion of Delegation
zones.sub: Delegation - warnings/limits - performance bottlenecks

enemies.lore: Delegation pushes processing to the source; non-delegable functions run locally up to the limit.
Too many refreshes and lookups are the most common performance bottleneck.
A delegation warning means the query may not return all rows.

boss.lore: Prefer delegable functions and data sources so the server filters and sorts data.

zones.name: Observatory of Monitor
zones.sub: App load time - Monitor - OnStart optimization - Application Insights

enemies.lore: Optimize App.OnStart; move work to OnVisible or Concurrent to speed startup.
Connector limits and throttling can slow or block data calls.
Monitor traces every event; Application Insights logs telemetry for diagnostics.

boss.lore: Use Monitor and Application Insights to find and fix performance and errors.

zones.name: Nexus of the Platform
zones.sub: Power Apps/Automate/BI/Pages/Copilot - connectors - Dataverse - CDM - when to code

enemies.lore: Connectors link Power Platform to external services and data.
The Common Data Model gives Dataverse standard tables and semantics.
Write code only when configuration cannot meet the requirement.

boss.lore: Power Platform = Power Apps + Automate + BI + Pages + Copilot Studio over Dataverse.

zones.name: Workshop of Tools
zones.sub: Power Platform CLI - VS tools - ALM - solutions (managed/unmanaged, layering)

enemies.lore: The Power Platform CLI (pac) scaffolds PCF, manages solutions and auth.
Solutions layer; managed layers sit above the unmanaged base.
Solution dependencies must exist in the target environment to import.

boss.lore: Package with SolutionPackager/CLI; managed to deploy, unmanaged to develop.

zones.name: Forge of Extensibility
zones.sub: UX extensibility (PCF, client script) - Dataverse API/plug-ins - Custom API - configure vs code

enemies.lore: The event pipeline (pre-validation, pre/post-operation) is where plug-ins run.
A Custom API defines a reusable, bound or unbound Dataverse operation.
Prefer business rules/flows over client script/plug-ins when they suffice.

boss.lore: Extend Dataverse via Web API, Organization Service, plug-ins and Custom APIs.

zones.name: Atrium of Client Script
zones.sub: Web resources - event handlers - execution/form context - common tasks

enemies.lore: Register client scripts on form/field events via form properties or code.
The execution context exposes the form context to access data and UI.
getControl/setVisible show or hide form controls, tabs and sections.

boss.lore: Client scripting (JavaScript) reacts to form events using the Client API context.

zones.name: Spire of the Xrm Object
zones.sub: Xrm global objects (Navigation, Utility, WebApi) - best practices - standards

enemies.lore: Xrm.WebApi performs CRUD against Dataverse from client script.
Avoid unsupported/deprecated methods; run the Solution Checker.
Define unique function names to avoid clashes across libraries.

boss.lore: The Xrm object exposes App, Navigation, Utility and WebApi for global operations.

zones.name: Foundry of Components
zones.sub: PCF advantages - component types - lifecycle - manifest - tooling

enemies.lore: The manifest (ControlManifest) declares a component's properties and resources.
PCF lifecycle: init, updateView, getOutputs, destroy.
Creator Kit and community components speed up building UIs.

boss.lore: The Power Apps component framework builds reusable code components for apps.

zones.name: Laboratory of Code Components
zones.sub: Create/build a PCF - manifest - styling - logic - package - test harness

enemies.lore: pac pcf init plus npm build compiles a code component project.
CSS web resources style a code component's rendered output.
Test and debug components in the Power Apps component test harness.

boss.lore: Implement updateView/getOutputs, package into a solution and deploy the component.

zones.name: Crypt of Dataverse
zones.sub: Extensibility model - plug-ins (.NET assemblies) - event framework/pipeline - sync vs async

enemies.lore: Dataverse is metadata-driven and solution-aware.
Event messages (Create, Update...) flow through the event pipeline stages.
Steps run synchronously (immediate) or asynchronously (background).

boss.lore: Extend Dataverse with .NET plug-ins registered on pipeline events and messages.

zones.name: Vault of Plug-ins
zones.sub: When to use plug-ins - execution context - pre/post images - shared variables

enemies.lore: InputParameters/OutputParameters carry data in the plug-in context.
Pre/PostEntityImages capture row values before and after the operation.
Prefer business rules or flows when a plug-in is not required.

boss.lore: A plug-in implements IPlugin.Execute and reads IPluginExecutionContext.

zones.name: Gateway of the Web API
zones.sub: Web API vs Organization Service - OData 4 - Entra ID auth (OAuth) - CRUD

enemies.lore: The Dataverse Web API is an OData v4 RESTful endpoint.
Register an app in Microsoft Entra ID and use OAuth to get an access token.
FetchXML expresses complex queries against Dataverse.

boss.lore: Call Dataverse over HTTP with OData and OAuth bearer tokens from Entra ID.

zones.name: Nexus of Azure
zones.sub: Azure integration options - Service Bus - registering endpoints/steps - listeners

enemies.lore: Azure Service Bus queues and relays Dataverse messages to external listeners.
Event Grid and Event Hubs handle event routing at scale.
Register a Service Bus endpoint and a step to publish Dataverse events.

boss.lore: Publish Dataverse data to Azure via Service Bus, Functions and messaging.

zones.name: Reactor of Functions
zones.sub: Functions vs Logic Apps/WebJobs - hosting plans - scaling - timeout

enemies.lore: The Consumption plan scales to zero; Premium avoids cold starts.
Choose Consumption, Flex, Premium or Dedicated hosting per need.
Function apps have a time-out duration that varies by plan.

boss.lore: Azure Functions run event-driven serverless code that scales on demand.

zones.name: Assembly of Bindings
zones.sub: Local dev/test - triggers and bindings - identity-based connections

enemies.lore: A trigger starts a function; bindings connect input/output data.
Binding direction is in, out or inout.
Use identity-based (managed identity) connections instead of secrets.

boss.lore: Define triggers and bindings to wire functions to Azure services.

zones.name: Portal of Connectors
zones.sub: Maker portal - general info - actions/triggers - request/response - validation

enemies.lore: Action and trigger naming shapes the connector's operations.
Action visibility (none/advanced/internal/important) controls exposure.
Request and response definitions describe the operation payloads.

boss.lore: Build a custom connector in a solution via the maker portal and an OpenAPI definition.

zones.name: Workshop of the Connector
zones.sub: Connected Services in VS - APIM - connector vs connection - OpenAPI import

enemies.lore: Add Power Platform as a connected service in Visual Studio.
Export a Web API from Azure API Management to a custom connector.
A connector is the definition; a connection is an authenticated instance.

boss.lore: Create custom connectors from VS, APIM or an OpenAPI (Swagger) definition.

zones.name: Citadel of Authentication
zones.sub: APIM gateway - sharing - auth options (Basic, API key, OAuth2, Windows) - Entra ID

enemies.lore: No-auth connectors expose the API without credentials.
API key and Basic authentication pass simple credentials.
OAuth 2.0 with Microsoft Entra ID delegates secure access.

boss.lore: Secure connectors with OAuth 2.0/Entra ID over an API Management gateway.

zones.name: Hall of Policies
zones.sub: Apply policies - expressions/runtime values - data conversion - host URL/routing

enemies.lore: Policy templates transform requests and responses without code.
Policy expressions read runtime values (headers, query, body).
Set host URL and route request policies redirect the call.

boss.lore: Connector policies reshape traffic: set values, convert data, route requests.

zones.name: Archive of OpenAPI
zones.sub: Swagger editor - chunk transfer - test connection - dynamic values/schema

enemies.lore: The dynamic list of values extension populates choices at runtime.
Dynamic schema adapts the payload shape to the operation.
Enable chunk transfer for large payloads.

boss.lore: OpenAPI extensions add dynamic values/schema, test connection and chunking.

zones.name: Foundry of Solutions
zones.sub: Connection references - environment variables - managed/unmanaged - deploy - DevOps

enemies.lore: Connection references and environment variables externalize configuration.
Unmanaged solutions are for dev; managed for downstream environments.
Power Platform Build Tools automate solution export/import in DevOps.

boss.lore: Manage solutions with connection references, env variables and ALM pipelines.

zones.name: Citadel of ALM
zones.sub: Solution layering - managed layers - updates/upgrades - source control - CLI unpack

enemies.lore: Managed layers stack over the unmanaged base; the top layer wins.
Managed updates patch; upgrades replace and remove deleted components.
pac solution unpack turns a solution zip into source-control-friendly files.

boss.lore: Architect solutions with layering, version control and CLI unpack for healthy ALM.

zones.name: Tower of the Exam
zones.sub: Full PL-400 question bank

enemies.lore: The exam covers the WHOLE PL-400 syllabus.

boss.lore: The final exam mixes questions from all 6 parts. Prove your mastery!

pages: Imperative versus declarative development — This module includes some concepts that might appear to be very developer focused but don't worry. The goal is to help you understand and apply some of these concepts to build better apps.

Imperative versus declarative — There are two main ways to approach development: Imperative development focuses on how to achieve a goal. Declarative development focuses on what result you want. Imperative development gives you more control because you define each step of the process. That flexibility often comes with more complexity. Declarative development is simpler and easier to read, but it can limit how much control you have over execution.

Imperative — To better understand imperative programming think about the sandwich that you want for lunch. In imperative programming, you focus on creating the sandwich in your "code." You go to the kitchen, get the ingredients, put the sandwich together, and then send it to the user. You spend numerous time on the steps, but you have all of the specific functions you want to make it exactly the way you want. No tomatoes? No problem. In a completely made up programming language, your code might look something like this.

Function Create Sandwich { Go to kitchen; Get ingredients; Remove tomato; Assemble sandwich;} Function Send Sandwich { Destination Mouth;} As you can see, there are lots of steps in the process, but you get a sandwich exactly the way you want. This is the approach you see in languages like C# or other popular coding languages. The focus is on pushing the data.

Declarative — For declarative programming, think of the same scenario, your sandwich for lunch. The difference is now you're focused on producing the sandwich, not how to make a sandwich. This is much less complex, but you might also run into the issue with tomatoes. If the function you use to get the sandwich doesn't support the option of no tomato you're out of luck. Your code may be as straight forward as follows.

GetSandwich(Kitchen, Mouth) That made up function takes two inputs, where to get the sandwich from and where to send it. There was no option to remove tomatoes. It would be up to the creator of the GetSandwich function to add an option for no Tomato, which might look like this. GetSandwich(Kitchen, Mouth, {Tomato: false}) Low-code tools like Excel use this approach to development. The focus is on pulling data.

Power Apps supports both imperative and declarative methodologies — Power Apps has capabilities for both imperative and declarative logic. Throughout this training, there has been a focus on declarative formulas. In the remainder of this module, the focus is on imperative concepts and methodologies in Power Apps. The key component of imperative logic in Power Apps is variables. The next unit covers the different types of variables and how to use them in an imperative mode.

The three types of variables in Power Apps — In Power Apps, you can use variables to temporarily store information that you need to reference while an app is running. Common scenarios include keeping a running count, managing UI state, improving performance by avoiding repeated calculations, or temporarily storing lists of data. Variables are a key driver for imperative logic in Power Apps because they allow you to "build the sandwich" piece by piece. To support you in these needs, Power Apps has three different types of variables.

Global variables -- The most traditional type of variable. You use the Set function to create and set its value. Then you can reference its values anywhere within your app. A common use is to store a user's DisplayName when the app loads and then reference the variable throughout the app. Context variables -- A context variable is only available on the screen where you create it using the UpdateContext function.

checks.q: A colleague says their canvas app logic 'defines each step to reach the goal, trading simplicity for fine-grained control.' Which development style is that?
checks.why: Imperative development focuses on HOW: you define each step and gain control at the cost of complexity. Declarative focuses on WHAT result you want.

checks.q: You must store the signed-in user's DisplayName once at launch and read it from any screen. Which function creates that value?
checks.why: Set creates a GLOBAL variable readable anywhere in the app. UpdateContext is screen-scoped; Collect/Patch write to data sources/collections.

checks.q: Decide whether each statement about Power Apps variables is true.
checks.why: UpdateContext can set many variables at once and is screen-scoped; Set defines one global variable per call, and globals are the flexible default.

rows.s: A context variable is available only on the screen where UpdateContext created it.
UpdateContext can declare several context variables in a single call.
The Set function can declare multiple global variables in one call, separated by commas.
Global variables are generally the recommended default when in doubt.

checks.q: Which of the following are genuine Power Apps variable mechanisms? (choose 2)
checks.why: Power Apps uses global variables (Set), context variables (UpdateContext) and collections. Environment variables belong to solutions/Dataverse, and there are no typed module constants in Power Fx.

check.q: A colleague says their canvas app logic 'defines each step to reach the goal, trading simplicity for fine-grained control.' Which development style is that?
check.why: Imperative development focuses on HOW: you define each step and gain control at the cost of complexity. Declarative focuses on WHAT result you want.

pages: With a declarative mindset, you would set the Visible property for the warning icon to the following. CountRows(Filter(InvoiceEntity, CustomerNumber = ThisCustomersNumber And Status = "Outstanding")) > 3 If that's true, then the icon displays, and if it's false the icon doesn't display. You would then repeat that same formula on the Visible property of the Approval button.

The problem is this becomes a complex formula that you maintain in two different locations, and that query will generate duplicate network traffic, processing in the app, and processing on the data source. A better approach is only to run the complex call once, store the result in a variable, and then use that variable to control the Visible property of each control. To do this, configure the OnVisible property of the screen to set the variable.

Set(varOustandingExceeded, CountRows(Filter(InvoiceEntity, CustomerNumber = ThisCustomersNumber And Status = "Outstanding")) > 3) The variable varOutstandingExceeded is either true or false based on the result of the formula. Now set the Visible property of the icon and button control to varOutstandingExceeded. No additional formula or functions are necessary. This is because those controls accept either true or false for the Visible property and the variable will be either true or false.

Based on your Set function in the OnVisible property of the screen, Power Apps will set the type of variable to Boolean and set the value to true or false based on the result of the formula. Small changes like this make your app both more performant and easier to maintain. You should incorporate variables anytime you're repetitively retrieving information that isn't going to change while you're using it.

Contextual variables — Contextual variables are similar to global variables except they're only referenced on the screen where you create them. Although it’s possible to set the user's name to a variable to reference throughout your app, there are still advantages to the fact that contextual variables can't be used on other screens. Sometimes you have functionality you want to use on multiple screens that is variable driven. For example, many apps use pop-up dialog boxes to confirm things like deleting a record.

A common way to implement is to set a Contextual variable to true when the user selects the delete button. You do that by setting the OnSelect property of the button to the following. UpdateContext({varShowPopUp: true}) You then set the Visible property of the pop-up controls to varShowPopUp. This is similar to the example from the global variables. The major difference is reusability. If you copy the controls (using Ctrl+C) to another screen, then you have two instances of varShowPopUp.

These two instances use the same name, but can have different values. The value of varShowPopUp on screen1 doesn't affect the value of varShowPopUp on screen2 because each contextual variable, even when they have the same name, are scoped to the screen they are on. Typically reusing variable names isn't recommended because it can be confusing, but it’s great if you want to reuse functionality independently on different screens.

If you are in doubt about whether you should use global or contextual variables, typically global variables are the default answer. Global variables are available everywhere, making them the most flexible. One unique behavior of the UpdateContext function is that you can declare more than one variable at a time. This isn't possible with the Set function. To create more than one context variable with a single formula, use a comma between the variables. UpdateContext({varCount: 1, varActive: true, varName:

User().FullName}) Note In the previous module, we mentioned that using the User() function inline will unnecessarily slow down your app and for that reason a global variable should be used OnStart. While using a contextual variable to store User information improves performance over inline use of the User() function, it's ultimately less performative than a global variable, as you'll still be calling on the data source each time that page opens.

checks.q: The same expensive CountRows(Filter(...)) drives the Visible of both an icon and a button. What is the more performant, maintainable fix?
o: Run it once in the screen OnVisible, store the boolean in a variable, and bind both controls to that variable
Duplicate the formula on each control's Visible property
Move the formula into the app OnStart only
Wrap each formula in a separate ForAll
checks.why: Computing once into a variable avoids duplicate network/app/data-source work and keeps the logic in one place.

checks.q: You copy controls that use UpdateContext({varShowPopUp:true}) from Screen1 to Screen2. What happens to varShowPopUp?
o: Each screen keeps its own independent value under the same name
Both screens now share one synchronized value
The paste fails because context names must be unique
The variable is promoted to a global automatically
checks.why: Context variables are scoped per screen, so identically named ones on different screens hold independent values.

checks.q: For reading the user's name repeatedly, which option is the MOST performant?
o: A global variable set once in OnStart
A context variable set on each screen's OnVisible
Calling User() inline wherever it's needed
Recomputing it in a collection per screen
checks.why: A global set once in OnStart avoids re-hitting the data source; a context variable still queries each time the page opens, and inline User() is the slowest.

checks.q: Decide whether each statement is true when a variable stores a boolean like varOutstandingExceeded.
checks.why: Power Apps types variables implicitly from the assigned value; a boolean variable binds straight to Visible.

rows.s: A control's Visible property accepts the boolean variable directly, needing no extra formula.
Power Apps infers the variable's type (Boolean) from the value you assign.
You must explicitly declare the variable's data type before using it.

check.q: The same expensive CountRows(Filter(...)) drives the Visible of both an icon and a button. What is the more performant, maintainable fix?
o: Run it once in the screen OnVisible, store the boolean in a variable, and bind both controls to that variable
Duplicate the formula on each control's Visible property
Move the formula into the app OnStart only
Wrap each formula in a separate ForAll
check.why: Computing once into a variable avoids duplicate network/app/data-source work and keeps the logic in one place.

pages: Variables can self-reference — This concept applies to both global and context variables. Sometimes you need to make a variable that points to itself. This is often done when you want to either do a counter type variable where it increments a value or you're appending a string. With Power Apps this is easy to implement. Place the following formula on the OnSelect property of a button to set up a counter.

Set(varCounter, varCounter + 1) When the app first starts, the value of varCounter is 0, and it's incremented by 1 each the time the button is selected. It's important to remember that the default value of a variable varies based on the variable type if you don't set the default property. Text variables are "" Number variables are 0 Boolean variables are false

A variable can store a single record — This concept applies to global and context variables. Collections differ slightly because they're a table made up of one or more records, meaning storing and retrieving a record is different for a collection. In the previous units, you learned how to store a single value in a global or context variable. You can also store a record in the variable. When you do this, you can then reference the different fields or columns by using the dot (.) notation.

In this example, you'll store the entire user record in a global variable named varUser. To do so, use the following function. Set(varUser, User()) This stores the entire user record in the variable. The user record has three columns Email, FullName, and Image. You can retrieve the values of the individual columns using the dot (.) notation. To display the user's email address, add a Label control to the screen and set the text property to:

varUser.Email This example stores the record from an action-based data source. You could also use the LookUp function as a way to retrieve and store a record from a tabular data source, like Microsoft Dataverse, in a variable.

Variables don't auto update — A common point of confusion for people who are new to variables is that variables don't automatically update. For example, they can use a variable to store the number of customer invoices using OnStart for the app. Then in the app, the user creates a new invoice. The variable doesn't distinguish the number of invoices in the system that have changed. The variable will only update when: The user closes the app and then opens it again. This causes OnStart to perform the operation to calculate the number of invoices.
You implement functionality to update the variable after the user creates an invoice. Be aware of this common point of confusion if you're new to using variables to track data.

Exercise - Using the variables and collections — This hands-on lab shows you how to create and use Global, Contextual, and Collection functions. Sign into the Power Apps maker portal (make.powerapps.com). From the Home screen left-side navigation panel, select + Create, then select Blank app and in the Create popup window under Blank canvas app select Create. Name your app Collection exercise, leave the format as Tablet, and select Create. After a few moments, your new app will appear. You can skip past the welcome dialog (if it appears).

In the Tree view panel, find and select the App object, then select the OnStart property. Set the OnStart property to this formula that we're using to create a collection of customer numbers and the associated invoice numbers (just copy and paste it into the fx formula bar): PowerApps Formula ClearCollect(colCustomer, {CustomerNumber: 7470, InvoiceNumber: "INV70817"}, {CustomerNumber: 4259, InvoiceNumber: "INV29595"}, {CustomerNumber: 8251, InvoiceNumber: "INV74302"}, {CustomerNumber: 2338, InvoiceNumber:

checks.q: Set(varCounter, varCounter + 1) sits on a button's OnSelect. Before it is ever set, what is varCounter's starting value?
checks.why: An uninitialized number variable defaults to 0, so the first click makes it 1.

checks.q: After Set(varUser, User()), how do you show the user's email in a label?
checks.why: A variable can hold a whole record; you read its columns with dot notation, e.g. varUser.Email.

checks.q: A variable counts invoices via OnStart. A user adds an invoice but the count doesn't change. Why?
o: Variables don't auto-update; the value only changes if you recompute it or the app restarts
OnStart runs continuously and should refresh it
checks.why: Variables are not reactive to data-source changes; you must update them explicitly or on the next OnStart.

checks.q: Decide the default value of each uninitialized variable type.
checks.why: Defaults are "" for text, 0 for number and false for boolean.

rows.s: A text variable defaults to an empty string "".
A number variable defaults to 0.
A boolean variable defaults to true.

check.q: Set(varCounter, varCounter + 1) sits on a button's OnSelect. Before it is ever set, what is varCounter's starting value?
check.why: An uninitialized number variable defaults to 0, so the first click makes it 1.

p1m2_0.t: Sometimes you need something more than forms
pages: Sometimes you need something more than forms — When building canvas apps in Power Apps, you go to Galleries to display records from your data source and Forms to view, create, and edit an individual record, but sometimes forms are not enough. In those scenarios, Power Apps has functions for updating your tabular data sources directly.

Directly create and edit a record — In this module, you will learn about using the Patch function to update your data sources without the use of forms directly. Patch is most often used when you need to take action on the data without user interaction in a repetitive manner, or your app design doesn't allow for the use of forms. For example, if you want to update a logging data source every time a user clicks a button to navigate to another screen you could use the formula for the OnSelect property of the button.

Patch(LoggingTable, Defaults(LoggingTable), {WhoClicked: User().FullName, WhenClicked: Now()}); Navigate(NextScreen, ScreenTransition.Cover) This formula would create a new record in the data source named LoggingTable. The WhoClicked column sets to the FullName property of the user who is signed in, and the WhenClicked column sets to the Date and Time of when they clicked the button. This would open the screen named NextScreen using the Cover screen transition.

Delete a record — There are also functions available for deleting one or more records from your data source. Those functions are: Remove and RemoveIf - These functions are used to remove or delete records from the data source. Clear - Use the Clear function to remove all of the records from a collection.

For example, if you wanted to give the user the ability to delete a record from a Gallery control, add a Trash icon to the Gallery displaying the data source CustomerOrders and then set the OnSelect property of the icon to the following. Remove(CustomerOrders, ThisItem) This formula would delete the record for the item that was displaying the Trash icon from the CustomerOrders data source.
There would be no confirmation, so you might consider implementing a check or pop-up dialog to confirm that the user truly wants to delete the record.

Bulk changes to records — Patch and Remove are both functions that are used to affect one record. If you need to affect change on more than one record, there are two options: Use the ForAll function, which was covered in the previous module, to loop through a table of data and run a Patch or Remove function for each record in the table. Use the Collect function to write from one table to another. Each record of the source table is added as a separate record to the target table.
These topics are covered in other Power Apps learning paths and are not covered in this learning path.

Collections are data sources — It’s important to remember that these functions can use a collection as their target. Patch, Remove, and RemoveIf can all be used to modify both tabular data sources and collections. As you build more complex apps storing data in collections and working with those items is very common, these functions will be a big part of that manipulation. The remainder of this module will refer to updating a data source. Remember that a data source can either be a tabular data source or a collection unless stated otherwise.

checks.q: When is the Patch function the RIGHT choice over an edit form in a canvas app?
o: When you must change data programmatically without user interaction, or the design has no form
Whenever you display records in a gallery
Only for reading a single record's columns
Any time you navigate between screens
checks.why: Patch updates data sources directly, ideal for repetitive/programmatic writes or designs without forms.

checks.q: Which function removes ALL records from a collection while keeping its columns defined?
checks.why: Clear empties a collection but preserves its column structure; Remove/RemoveIf target specific rows.

checks.q: Decide whether each statement about Patch, Remove and collections is true.
checks.why: Patch/Remove act on one record; for many you loop with ForAll. Both work on collections and tables, and Remove gives no confirmation.

rows.s: Remove deletes a record without asking the user to confirm.
Patch, Remove and RemoveIf can target a collection as well as a tabular data source.
Patch and Remove can each modify many records at once on their own.

checks.q: You need to write each row of one table into another, one record per source row. Which approach fits?
o: ForAll looping a Patch/Remove, or Collect to copy rows between tables
checks.why: Bulk changes use ForAll (per-record Patch/Remove) or Collect to append each source row to a target table.

check.q: When is the Patch function the RIGHT choice over an edit form in a canvas app?
o: When you must change data programmatically without user interaction, or the design has no form
Whenever you display records in a gallery
Only for reading a single record's columns
Any time you navigate between screens
check.why: Patch updates data sources directly, ideal for repetitive/programmatic writes or designs without forms.

p1m2_1.t: Use Patch to edit a record
pages: "Asia", Country: "China"}) This formula updates the record with an ID of 1 in the CustomerOrders table by setting the Region column to Asia and the Country column to China. If there are existing values in those fields, it is overwritten.

Update columns with Patch — The primary logic of most Patch functions is updating the proper columns with the correct information. This is the source of most of your troubleshooting of the Patch function. Use the following points to help you work through Patch. Make sure you update all of the required columns from your data source. You can update as many or as few of the optional columns as you would like. Make sure your column names are spelled and capitalized correctly. Column names are case-sensitive.

Make sure you write the correct data type. For example, if your column in the data source is a number type, then you can't write a string value to it, even if that string contains a number. There are four sources to pass values in your formula to Patch your data source: You can hardcode a value. An example is if you want to patch the status of the record with "Pending," your Patch formula would look like: PowerApps Formula Patch(CustomerOrders, Default(CustomerOrders), {Status:

"Pending"}) This formula creates a new record and sets the Status column to the string value of "Pending." You can reference a variable. For example, you can store the string "Under Review" in a variable named varStatus with the following formula. PowerApps Formula Set(varStatus, "Under Review") Then your Patch formula would be: PowerApps Formula Patch(CustomerOrders, Default(CustomerOrders), {Status:

varStatus}) This formula creates a new record and sets the Status column to the string value of "Under Review." You can reference the value from the property of a control. An example would be setting the value from a drop-down menu named Dropdown1 that contained the regions. Your Patch formula would look like: PowerApps Formula Patch(CustomerOrders, Default(CustomerOrders), {Status:

Dropdown1.Selected.Value}) This formula creates a new record and sets the Status column to the value of the selected item in the drop-down menu. You can use the output of a formula. An example would be setting the value of the Owner column using the FullName from the User() function. Your Patch formula would look like: PowerApps Formula Patch(CustomerOrders, Default(CustomerOrders), {Owner:
User().FullName}) This formula creates a new record and sets the Owner column to the current user's FullName from Microsoft Entra ID.

Patch example — Let's take a look at another example, in this example you're trying to build a solution for signing users into class as they arrive. This type of Power Apps solution is common, and the Patch function helps you quickly achieve results.

Solution breakdown — Here we have a simple Canvas app connected to our data source (TrainingClassSignIn). The data source has the following columns, Training Class, FirstName, LastName, EmailAddress, SignInStatus. This is the information we want to capture when a user selects the Sign In button. In the formula bar, you see the following code:

checks.q: In Patch(CustomerOrders, Defaults(CustomerOrders), {Status:"Pending"}), what does Defaults() do?
o: Signals Patch to CREATE a new record
checks.why: Passing Defaults(DataSource) as the base record tells Patch to create a new row; passing an existing record edits it.

checks.q: Decide whether each Patch column rule is true.
checks.why: Patch needs correct casing, all required columns, and matching data types; optional columns are up to you.

rows.s: Column names in Patch are case-sensitive.
You must supply all REQUIRED columns of the data source.
You can write a string value into a number column as long as it looks numeric.
You may set as many or as few OPTIONAL columns as you want.

checks.q: Which are valid sources for the values you pass into a Patch change record? (choose 2)
o: A control property such as Dropdown1.Selected.Value
The output of a formula like User().FullName
The name of the screen you are on
checks.why: Patch values can come from hardcoded literals, variables, control properties or formula outputs.

checks.q: Patch(CustomerOrders, ThisItem, {Region:"Asia", Country:"China"}) is run on an existing row. What happens to prior Region/Country values?
o: They are overwritten with the new values
They are appended to a history log
The row is duplicated with the new values
Patch is rejected because the row exists
checks.why: Passing an existing record edits it in place, overwriting the specified columns.

check.q: In Patch(CustomerOrders, Defaults(CustomerOrders), {Status:"Pending"}), what does Defaults() do?
o: Signals Patch to CREATE a new record
check.why: Passing Defaults(DataSource) as the base record tells Patch to create a new row; passing an existing record edits it.

pages: Delete all of the records — It's also possible to delete all of the records in a data source. This is most common with collections where you can use the Clear function. If you want to delete all of the records from a data source, you can use RemoveIf.

Delete all of the records in a collection — The Clear function deletes all the records of a collection. The columns of the collection remain. The only input you pass to the function is the collection name. For example, you could use the following formula to delete all of the records from a collection called collectSelectedItems. PowerApps Formula Clear(collectSelectedItems) This formula deletes all of the records from the collectSelectedItems collection without changing the columns of the collection.

You typically see this type of formula when you want to clear out the collection without having to redefine it, like in the case of a reset button or selecting a new order. When working on collections, you also have the ClearCollect function. The ClearCollect function deletes all the records from a collection and then adds a different set of records to the same collection. With a single function, ClearCollect offers the combination of Clear and then Collect. All three functions have their place.

One way to think about whether you want to use Clear and Collect versus ClearCollect is when the clearing of the collection happens, compared to when you want to add records back. Here are two examples to illustrate: All at once - For example, if you're reloading the items in a collection for a drop-down menu when a screen becomes visible, you would want to use ClearCollect. A single ClearCollect function in your formula removes the old records and immediately adds the new records.

Multi-step - For example, if you're using collections to store user inputs like in a shopping cart you can use Clear and Collect. This is because the user might want to clear their shopping cart without adding a new record.

Delete all of the records from a data source — It's possible to delete all of the records from a data source using RemoveIf. This isn't a common scenario. Again there's no confirmation before the formula processes unless you build such functionality. Finally, there's no undo or recycle bin in Power Apps. If you want to recover your data, you would need to go to your data source and use whatever recovery process is available for that data source, outside of Power Apps. Proceed with caution.

For example, you could use the following formula to delete all of the records from a data source. PowerApps Formula RemoveIf(CustomerOrders, true) This formula deletes all of the records from the CustomerOrders data source without changing the columns of the data source. The reason this works is RemoveIf checks every record in the data source to see if the equation equals true. In this case, the equation is set to true, so every record is deleted.

Note Setting the equation portion to true also works with the Filter function. This can be a valuable setting if you are trying to troubleshoot formulas where you are not sure if Filter is returning data.

Use the Patch function to update a Gallery — This hands-on lab shows you how to use the Patch function in a gallery. Sign in to Power Apps. From the Home screen select + Create from the left-hand navigation panel, then select Blank app and then Create under Blank canvas app. Name your app "Patch Exercise" or another appropriate title of your choice and select Create. Select the Insert button and add a Button control, and set its OnSelect property to this formula:

checks.q: You must empty a data source's records AND immediately reload a fresh set into a collection in one step. Which function is designed for that?
o: Clear then a separate Collect on a timer
checks.why: ClearCollect combines Clear and Collect in a single call, ideal for reloading a collection all at once.

checks.q: For a shopping-cart collection where the user may empty the cart WITHOUT immediately adding new items, which pattern fits best?
o: Clear now and Collect later as separate steps
checks.why: Multi-step scenarios (clear now, add later) use separate Clear and Collect; all-at-once reloads use ClearCollect.

checks.q: RemoveIf(CustomerOrders, true) is executed. What is the result?
o: Every record is deleted because the condition is always true
Nothing, because true is not a valid filter
Only the first record is deleted
The columns are removed but rows remain
checks.why: RemoveIf tests each row against the condition; a condition of true matches all rows, deleting them (no undo).

checks.q: Decide whether each statement about deleting data is true.
checks.why: There is no undo in Power Apps for deletes; recovery depends on the data source. A true condition is a handy Filter debug trick.

rows.s: Power Apps provides a built-in undo / recycle bin for records deleted via RemoveIf.
Setting the condition to true also works with the Filter function for troubleshooting.

check.q: You must empty a data source's records AND immediately reload a fresh set into a collection in one step. Which function is designed for that?
o: Clear then a separate Collect on a timer
check.why: ClearCollect combines Clear and Collect in a single call, ideal for reloading a collection all at once.

pages: Introduction — A common requirement in business app data storage to help ensure data consistency is a data column with a standardized, infrequently changing list of values. Users enter data by choosing an option from a list instead of typing a free-form value. Examples of these options include lists such as countries/regions, incident priority, and satisfaction rating. Microsoft Dataverse has a column type that supports this functionality, the Choice column.

Choice columns allow a user to pick values from a list when entering data. By default, users can pick a single choice, but the column can be easily configured to allow multiple values from a single list of known values. For example, Primary Category could be a single choice column, Other Categories could be a multiple choice column, and both could use the same list of categories. You can either use system-defined values or a custom list of values for choice columns.

The choices are stored as table column metadata and can only be modified by an app maker, not an app user. This feature is beneficial when building an app that works with a list of values, as your formulas can reference the choice list name and the display names of the values. For example, the following formula sets a color on a gallery item's text Color property based on the Category column, which is a choice column.

As you compose this formula, Power Apps will know the possible values for the Category column, and will allow you to select the value to compare from the list of known values for the choice column as soon as you insert the "." after the column name. Note If you add or change values on a choice list and the new or changed choices don’t immediately show in the editor, refresh the table in the data panel.
Refresh the table on the data panel by selecting the ellipsis (...) next to the table, and selecting Refresh from the pop-up menu. Repeat until your new values show.

Local versus global — You can create a list of available choice values as a local or global list. Local choice lists can only be used in the column and table where they’re created. The Global choice option allows the list to be used in multiple columns, either in the same table or in many different tables. When you create a new Choice column, the default value for the option Sync with global choice? is Yes (which is recommended). The Global choice option allows use of the same choices in other tables.

Unless the values only apply to a single column in a single table, use global choice values. With Yes selected, notice how the Sync this choice with becomes a mandatory field, and you must make a selection for the choices. When you select this field, a pane showing the various options available will appear next to the new column pane. You can either type in the choice-type you're looking for, or scroll through the list to select one.

Also, notice that when you hover over any of the choice sets, you get a preview of the choices that are already part of that choice set. It's also possible to create a new choice set by selecting the + New choice, and by entering information on your choices. Notice that you can also assign a Default choice for your column as you create it. Don't worry, you can come back later and set or change the default choice.

If you determine that you want to keep your choice options as a local choice set, you would select No under Sync with global choice?. In this case, you would define your choices just below the No button. (At least one is required.) You'll just need to add a Label for the choice; Power Apps will automatically assign an integer value for that item. You can change the value, but it's not recommended. You can also select a color for each choice by selecting the box just to the left of the Label name.

checks.q: Where are the values of a Dataverse choice column stored, and who can change them?
o: As table column metadata, editable only by an app maker
As ordinary rows any app user can edit
In a local collection on each screen
checks.why: Choice values live in column metadata and can only be modified by a maker, not end users.

checks.q: You create a Choice column and leave 'Sync with global choice?' at its default. What is that default and its effect?
o: Yes, so the same choices can be reused in other columns and tables
No, so the choices stay local to this one column
Yes, but the list becomes read-only forever
No, and multiple selection is disabled
checks.why: The default is Yes (global), letting the choice set be reused elsewhere; local (No) restricts it to that column/table.

checks.q: Decide whether each statement about choice columns is true.
checks.why: Choices support single/multiple selection, integer-backed labels, and global reuse; only makers edit the list.

rows.s: A choice column can be configured to allow multiple selected values.
You should use global choices unless the values apply to a single column in a single table.
For a local choice, you must define each label and Power Apps assigns it an integer value.
End users can add new options to a choice list at run time.

checks.q: Before creating a choice column, what should most influence your decision to use a choice vs a lookup?
o: How the apps, automation and reporting will consume the data
The color you want each option to display
The number of screens in the app
Whether the app is tablet or phone layout
checks.why: Choose choice vs lookup based on how the data will be used downstream by apps, flows and reports.

check.q: Where are the values of a Dataverse choice column stored, and who can change them?
o: As table column metadata, editable only by an app maker
As ordinary rows any app user can edit
In a local collection on each screen
check.why: Choice values live in column metadata and can only be modified by a maker, not end users.

pages: So before you create the column, consider how the apps, automation, or reporting will be using the data. The rest of this module explores how to use choice columns when you're building a canvas app from Microsoft Power Apps.

Filter Dataverse choice columns with Power Fx formulas — When you have a Dataverse table with a choice column, you’ll often want to filter data using that choice column. The most common filtering scenarios are: Filter the table rows for display in a gallery. Have a dropdown menu or combo box control with the list of choice values, and then let the user select one or more. Then, you can use the selected values to filter the table rows that you show in the gallery.

For example, if you have a Category choice column on the Accounts table, you can use the following logic to filter only preferred customers: PowerApps Formula Filter( Accounts, Category = 'Category (Accounts)'.'Preferred Customer' Be sure to use the full table.column string, and not just the column string, 'Preferred Customer' (like the image below), because it's an invalid comparison. If you forget to add the table to the string, you see the 'Incompatible types for comparison' error.

Since the 'Category (Accounts)' is a table (or option set) itself, you can't compare the table value with a text value. Therefore, you need to use the fully qualified reference: 'Category (Accounts)'.'Preferred Customer' (also displayed as [@Category].'Preferred Customer'). Frequently, you use a dropdown or combo box to filter a gallery so users can select which categories of items to display.

In the following example, you'll use a combo box to allow users to make multiple selections of account categories to show in the gallery. First, add a combo box to the screen, and then set the Items property by using the Choices function. The Choices() function prepares a list of values for your user to select from by using the metadata for the choice column Accounts.Category.

Filter(Accounts, Category in ComboBoxCategory.SelectedItems) Using the in operator allows the formula to filter on any of the selected categories. This formula will only show rows in the gallery when at least one category is selected. If you want to show all rows when no categories are selected, you could add an IsEmpty check to your formula. PowerApps Formula Filter( Accounts, Category in ComboBoxCategory.SelectedItems || IsEmpty(ComboBoxCategory.SelectedItems)

Filter choices columns — Filtering table rows on a choices column is complicated by how the data is stored in Dataverse as a comma-separated list. As a result, any filter that you compose that involves a choices column receives a delegation warning, as illustrated in the following example. One approach that you could take to avoid the delegation issue is to create a Dataverse table view that filters the choices values and then use the view in your Filter() function criteria.

This approach would help you avoid the delegation warning, but it won't allow the app user to provide the filter criteria values. The following screenshot shows an example of a Dataverse view filter for the Preferred Delivery choices column. You could use this Dataverse view named Monday Delivery by using the following Filter() function: PowerApps Formula Filter( Accounts, 'Accounts (Views)'.'Monday Delivery' Additionally, you can still include user-entered criteria for columns other than the Category.

For example, the following Filter() function shows the addition of the Category choice column, which is filtered on the value that the user selected from the dropdown list. PowerApps Formula Filter( Accounts, 'Accounts (Views)'.'Monday Delivery', Category = Dropdown1.Selected.Value Because of their standardized list of values, choice and choices columns are useful in providing consistent ways to filter table rows.

checks.q: Why must you write Category = 'Category (Accounts)'.'Preferred Customer' rather than Category = 'Preferred Customer'?
o: The choice is an option-set/table, so you need the fully qualified table.column reference
Short names only work in galleries
Power Fx forbids spaces in values
checks.why: A choice is itself an option set; comparing to a bare text gives an 'Incompatible types' error, so use the full reference.

checks.q: A combo box lets users pick several categories. Which formula shows gallery rows matching ANY selected category?
checks.why: The in operator filters on membership across the multiple selected items.

checks.q: To also show ALL rows when no category is selected, what do you add to Filter(Accounts, Category in ComboBoxCategory.SelectedItems ...)?
checks.why: Adding || IsEmpty(...SelectedItems) returns all rows when the user has selected nothing.

checks.q: Filtering on a multi-select 'choices' column raises a delegation warning. What is a recommended workaround?
o: Create a Dataverse view that filters the choices, then filter against that view
Increase the row limit to 2000 and ignore it
Store the choices in a global variable first
Convert the column to a text field
checks.why: Because 'choices' is stored as a comma-separated list (non-delegable), a Dataverse view that pre-filters avoids the warning.

check.q: Why must you write Category = 'Category (Accounts)'.'Preferred Customer' rather than Category = 'Preferred Customer'?
o: The choice is an option-set/table, so you need the fully qualified table.column reference
Short names only work in galleries
Power Fx forbids spaces in values
check.why: A choice is itself an option set; comparing to a bare text gives an 'Incompatible types' error, so use the full reference.
